secarb__001_a.text:
Applicant Professional
Allied Health Professional
Background
Profession (Allied
Physiotherapist
Health Professional)
Please describe your research career to date
I am recognised as one of the clinical leaders in the field of vestibular physiotherapy in the United Kingdom. I
believe I have the experience, ambition and motivation to successfully complete this doctoral fellowship and
become a future clinical academic leader in the specialty.
Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute
stroke population (January 2009–January 2011). This quantitative study, involving five patients, was presented as
a poster at the World Confederation for Physiotherapy (June 2011). The experience provided valuable insights into
the research process and sparked my passion for integrating research with clinical practice.
I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

secarb__001_b.text:
, analysing, and disseminating
research using quantitative and qualitative methodologies. My dissertation involved a mixed-methods survey of
physiotherapists’ management of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) in the UK, which required university
ethics approval. The findings were published in the Journal of Physiotherapy (September 2019) and presented as
posters at the European Region of World Confederation for Physiotherapy (November 2021).
Returning to my NHS role, I co-led a project identifying vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease patients, in
the largest cohort studied to date. This research was published in the Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and
Psychiatry (November 2018) and presented at the Bárány Society Meeting (June 2018). Building on these results,
we secured funding from The Lily Foundation (May 2019) for a prospective observational study. I co-developed a
diagnostic framework for vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease, supervised research associates,
managed data, and adapted the methodology during the COVID-19 pandemic. I created an electronic case record
form and coordinated research activities within the CPMS. I conducted a modified D

secarb__001_c.text:
elphi consensus for the
framework development, published in Journal of Neurology (June 2023) and shared as a poster at the Bárány
Society Meeting (May 2022). I led the dissemination of the framework’s sensitivity and specificity results in the
European Journal of Neurology (May 2024) and published a patient survey analysis in Audiology Research (June
2024).
In collaboration with the acute vertigo service, I co-authored a publication highlighting the prevalence of central
versus peripheral causes of positional vertigo and common diagnostic errors by non-specialists. These findings
were presented at the British Society of Neuro-Otology annual conference (October 2023).
My research endeavours have fuelled my interest in positional dizziness and equipped me with robust skills in
designing, conducting, and disseminating studies. Beyond my projects, I have peer-reviewed manuscripts for
various journals and co-edited a specialist supplement on dizziness and balance disorders for the Journal of
Laryngology and Otology (May 2024). I also co-authored a British Society of Audiology practice guideline on
vestibular rehabilitation (April 2019) and contributed two case studies to the book “Puzzl

secarb__001_d.text:
ing Cases in Vertigo and
Dizziness”.
Clinical and leadership experience
I have worked clinically as a vestibular and neurological physiotherapist since 2002, and have developed and led
three vestibular rehabilitation services, within secondary and tertiary NHS services and the private sector. I have
developed advanced clinical skills in vestibular assessment and treatment, through mentoring by medical and
vestibular specialists, independent professional development, and attendance at specialist courses in the UK and
overseas.
I was responsible for the supervision of junior colleagues, appraisals, and performance management. I have
supported team members to analyse data, present audits, develop service improvement ideas and report the
findings to managers. I have helped others design posters for conferences and pass these skills onto more junior
team members. In my last position, I supported the entire therapy outpatient department in a major transformation
project to adopt an online documentation system and coordinated two department relocations and changes in
service delivery.
My interest in vestibular rehabilitation as a speciality, led me to become one of the founding members of

secarb__001_e.text:
the
Chartered Society of Physiotherapy special interest group – the Association of Chartered Physiotherapist
interested in Vestibular Rehabilitation (ACPIVR). I was a committee member for eight years (2008-2016),
culminating in 2 years as vice chairperson, and the final two years as chairperson. This committee was
responsible for developing the knowledge and skills of other physiotherapists nationally, raising awareness of the
specialism, and developing resources for clinicians and patients.
I am passionate about providing evidence-based treatment and support others to do the same. I frequently
provide training locally, nationally and internationally, to physiotherapists and a variety of medical professionals on
vestibular rehabilitation and BPPV management. I lecture on several University College London (UCL) Master’s
programs and have supervised three Master’s student’s research projects, to include supporting one to publish
their findings in a peer reviewed journal.
Impact of Fellowship
I am privileged to have an honorary position with one of the world’s leading neuro-otology research units at UCL, a
testament to my standing as one of the UK’s most experienced and respected ves

secarb__001_f.text:
tibular physiotherapists. My
strong relationships within the multidisciplinary clinical and research community have been instrumental in
advancing patient care and contributing to impactful research.
Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application,

secarb__001_g.text:
or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
This research project was submitted to the Meniere’s Society, the UK’s balance and dizziness charity, to request
research funding. The application was peer reviewed by several external experts and layperson committee
members, and feedback was given to the trustees. A decision was given in December 2024 to award £55,106.04
towards the project. The award letter stated they felt the project was “…important and would be very beneficial for
patients with dizziness and unsteadiness”.
Unfortunately, as they are a small charity with limited funds and receive numerous grant applications each year,
they were unable to award the full amount requested. However, they are very enthusiastic about a joint
collaboration with the NIHR and fully support this research fellowship application. They are keen to help
throughout the project including with recruitment and dissemination via presentations, their newsletter and at their
balance awareness week events.
This fellowship application reflects the total cost of £447,139.00 that is required to undertake this research project,
remunerate

secarb__001_h.text:
patient and public involvement, undertake relevant professional and academic training, attend
conferences, and complete a three year full-time PhD. The amount awarded by the Meniere’s Society of
£55,106.04, will be deducted from the total cost, should this application be successful.
This application has also been reviewed by the NIHR research support service hub and underwent a
grantsmanship review. All feedback has been taken onboard and addressed and I believe this has strengthened
the application.
Contextual Factors
Please use this section to detail any factors that may have impacted the development of your research career or
outputs from your research career to date. NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take
them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
● Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
● Reduced time spent undertaking research activities (including research outputs), due to disability or to
caring responsibilities. This may include physical or mental health related impairments or conditions, which have
impacted your opportunities to undertake research activities
● Reduced op

secarb__001_i.text:
portunities for career support e.g., mentorship, and limited opportunities to undertake prior
research and training.
● Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding or fewer publications. In general terms,
contextual factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that the information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with the
General Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).
The last NHS position I held had no research time apportioned and the data collection phase of the observational
study we had received charity funding (Lily Foundation) for, had finished. There was a large amount of data that
required

secarb__001_j.text:
analysing, findings to share with the patients and public, and professional manuscripts to be written.
I am committed to progressing my career as a clinical academic researcher, and my obligations to disseminate
results from my research activities. As such, I made the decision to leave my full-time NHS position to allow time
to focus on my research and took up a part time, honorary (unpaid) contract role based in the SENSE research
unit, at UCL. This part time role has allowed the time to prepare and successfully publish three articles relating to
the Lily Foundation research grant and assist in one other. It also allowed time to publish one more article relating
to this fellowship topic and be an editor for a standalone supplement in the Journal of laryngology and otology.
Moreover, alongside the research team, I continue to analyse the data to address secondary variables.
In addition, this part time role in the SENSE research unit has provided a rich opportunity to collaborate and
network with current UCL PhD students and other distinguished researchers. It has provided time to undertake
the patient focus group, establish a patient advisory group for this project, and the time t

secarb__001_k.text:
o develop this fellowship
application to advance my academic career.
In addition to my professional ambitions, I have qualified for Great Britain’s Age Group Triathlon teams, for both
the European Championships (sprint distance) and World Championships (Olympic distance) in 2025. The last
competition takes place in Australia in October 2025, and this is why I have deferred the start date of this research
fellowship until January 2026 to allow these competitions to have concluded. I have the support of my supervisory
team, to ensure I will be able to manage this research fellowship in future, alongside the training for triathlon
events, so that I can pursue both my professional and sporting ambitions.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Title
Finding out what causes dizziness that is triggered when changing position, to speed up access to the right
treatment
Background to the research
Dizziness affects 1-in-3 people at some stage in their lives. The main type is dizziness when changing position,
such as getting into bed and rolling over. It can have a huge impact on a person’s ability to do everyday activities,
limiting how much they move or walk and can cause falls.
To get help, people visit doctors and physiotherapists in a range of healthcare settings including general
practitioner clinics, emergency departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
The main cause of dizziness when changing position, is due to small crystals getting lodged in a part of the inner
ear where they are not meant to be. When the person moves they experience dizziness due to the crystal
moving. Doctors and physiotherapists can relocate these crystals, by moving the person through a series of
specific positions. Sometimes, dizziness when changing position can be caused by brain problems but many
doctors and physiotherapists are less aware of this.
There are several problems doctors and physiotherapists face wh
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
en treating this type of dizziness, specifically:
 Many are unaware that conditions affecting the brain can also cause this type of dizziness and these
conditions need to be treated differently
 We do not know how many treatments are needed to cure an inner ear problem and how often it returns
 We do not know why some people do not get better
Aims of the research
Aim 1 - develop a list of the clinical features that suggest a brain cause of the dizziness
Aim 2 - identify how many treatments are needed and how often it comes back for an inner ear cause of the
dizziness
Aim 3 - explore if there are differences in the inner ear anatomy for those that do not get better with the specific
treatment positions
Design and methods used
Study 1 – I will complete a thorough review of all the research on brain causes of dizziness with position changes.
This information will be combined with the findings from study 2 to create a list of features to identify brain causes
of dizziness.
Study 2 – I will invite 250 people who have dizziness when they change position to take part. They will fill in
questionnaires, have a thorough dizziness assessment, and treatment when appropriate. Information on
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
the
number of treatments required, and recurrence will be collected.
Study 3 – I will invite 25 people that get better with treatment, and 25 people that do not get better, to have scans
of their inner ears. 3D models will be made from the scans and compared for any differences in the anatomy of
the inner ears.
Patient and public involvement
Ten people with experience of this type of dizziness, expressed frustrations in getting a diagnosis quickly, and
access to the right treatment. Their feedback has shaped the research question and study design, specifically:
 How to advertise the study
 Printing materials, instead of relying on email alone
 Providing interpreters
 Refunding travel costs
Another group of eight patients have agreed to help with recruitment and share the results widely.
Dissemination
Doctors and physiotherapists are the key audience to share these results with, to raise awareness of the causes
and treatment of dizziness caused by changing position, to improve diagnosis and treatment. This will be done
through publications in healthcare journals, presentations at international conferences and courses.
The results will also be shared with patients and public usin
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_d.text:
g social media and an infographic, newsletters and at a
specific workshop.
secpesor__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Background
Positional dizziness accounts for nearly 50% of dizziness cases seeking medical attention. It describes dizziness,
vertigo and unsteadiness triggered by changes in head position, and heavily impacts patients’ independence,
mood, and falls risk.
Patients present across a range of healthcare settings, including general practitioner’s clinics, emergency
departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
Most cases are due to five subtypes of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), but brain (central) disorders
such as tumours, stroke, or vestibular migraine are under-recognised causes of positional dizziness. It is
important to determine the cause to ensure expedient management because the treatments provided by doctors
and physiotherapists for BPPV will not work for central causes and they may require emergency treatment.
Patients in a focus group expressed frustrations at getting a diagnosis and access to prompt and effective
treatment, reinforcing the major challenges faced by doctors and physiotherapists in this area, specifically:
 poor awareness of the diagnostic features of central causes of positional dizziness
 limited evidence on th

secsa__001_b.text:
e number of treatments required to resolve each BPPV subtype and the recurrence
rates for each BPPV subtype
 uncertainty regarding reasons for poor treatment response and whether an anatomical variation of the
inner ear could explain this
This research intends to address these gaps in knowledge to refine diagnostic frameworks, improve access to
timely effective treatment by doctors and physiotherapists, and improve patient quality of life.
Aims and objectives
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

secsa__001_c.text:
izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment
chair, wearing video-goggles to record and quantify eye movements. Data will be collected on BPPV treatments
needed and recurrence rates.
Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV
treatment in study 2. Reconstruct three-dimensional models from magnetic resonance imaging scans of the inner
ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

secdrp__001_a.text:
Title:
Finding out what causes dizziness that is triggered when changing position, to speed up access to the right
treatment
This research fellowship aims to expand the limited literature to help doctors and physiotherapists identify and
distinguish between inner ear and brain causes of positional dizziness more effectively, where treatment and
outcomes differ substantially. The overall objective is to improve access to the correct diagnosis and treatment in
a more timely manner and thus enhance patient care and experience.
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Dizziness ranks among the top reasons for visiting a doctor, affecting up to 35% of individuals at some point in
their lives1. Dizziness that is triggered by a change in head position, for example, getting into bed, rolling in bed,
and bending over, is the main cause1,2. Such positional dizziness can result in dizziness and vertigo2, with
unsteadiness commonly reported3. The symptoms are typically brief but can have a significant impact on
activities4, confidence4.
independence in everyday negatively impacting mood and walking Of greatest concern in
the elderly, positional dizziness is associated with high falls risk4, wit

secdrp__001_b.text:
h associated morbidity and mortality4,5.
Patients with positional dizziness can present to doctors and physiotherapists working in a wide range of
healthcare settings, including general practitioners clinics, emergency departments, physiotherapy services, falls
services and specialist neuro-otology departments3,4,6.
Most cases of positional dizziness are due to an inner ear condition called benign paroxysmal positional vertigo
(BPPV), with the majority of occurrences easily cured1. However, a small proportion of cases are due to brain
disorders, that if left undetected and untreated can have life-threatening consequences1,7.
There is limited detailed prevalence data on positional dizziness across healthcare settings, with research failing to
differentiate between inner ear (peripheral) and brain (central) causes or reporting on just one cause in the study’s
population. The most comprehensive data reports in 100 patients that had position triggered dizziness, 80% had
BPPV and 20% had central causes7.
One challenge related to positional dizziness is that it can be due to peripheral or central disorders2. Bedside, gold
diagnosis3,8
standard positioning manoeuvres are needed to make th

secdrp__001_c.text:
e correct but these tests rely on correct
execution and interpretation of the induced eye movement (nystagmus). This can be challenging for doctors or
physiotherapists that have little or no training in managing dizziness9 where these patients may present.
It is important to identify whether positional dizziness is due to a peripheral or central disorder because treatments
differ. Repositioning manoeuvres treat and resolve most peripheral causes8. Whereas in central disorders these
do not work, and medication is typically used to reduce symptoms, alongside investigation to address underlying
sinister causes that may require urgent intervention (e.g. brain tumours)2,10.
Furthermore, when BPPV is identified, there is a lack of evidence to guide how many treatments are needed, what
the expected recurrence rates are, and why some patients do not get better.
This research fellowship aims to build on the existing literature and address these critical gaps in understanding,
which could refine diagnostic frameworks, and improve treatment outcomes. This aligns with patient priorities for
quicker, more accurate diagnoses and the broader need for evidence-based management of positional dizzin

secdrp__001_d.text:
ess.
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or wellbeing of the public and/or to
patients/service users, carers, and health and care services?
Positional dizziness is a prevalent complaint in healthcare1,3, yet it is frequently misdiagnosed6, and can have life-
threatening consequences1,7 . Frequent delays in treatment3,11 results in increased strain on healthcare
resources3,11 life3.
and greater impact on patient quality of By developing a practical and evidence-based
diagnostic and treatment framework, this programme of work has the potential to:
Enhance Diagnostic Accuracy: Equip clinicians with tools to differentiate peripheral from central causes of
positional dizziness10, ensuring patients receive timely and appropriate treatment.
Support Healthcare Providers: Provide clinicians with accessible, evidence-based tools to confidently diagnose
and manage positional dizziness, reducing misdiagnosis6 and unnecessary referrals11.
Improve Quality of Life: Accurate and early diagnosis and treatment will enable patients to return to work, live
independently, and fully engage with family and social activities3. This will significantly reduce the mental an

secdrp__001_e.text:
d
physical burden associated with positional dizziness, such as anxiety and social isolation.
Reduce Falls and Injury Risks: For elderly patients, positional dizziness is a major risk factor for falls4, which can
lead to severe injury or death4,5 either directly or through complications. Reducing fall risks will have a profound
impact on patient safety and long-term health outcomes.
Therefore, this research not only addresses immediate clinical needs but also has the potential to transform care
pathways, reduce healthcare costs, and optimise resource allocation within the NHS. By alleviating the burden of
dizziness on individuals and healthcare systems alike, this work anticipates meaningful, long-term impact on public
health and wellbeing.
3. Review of the existing evidence - how does the existing literature support this proposal?
What causes positional dizziness?
Positional dizziness can result from either an inner ear (peripheral) cause, or due to a condition affecting the brain
(central).
The commonest cause of peripheral positional dizziness is BPPV, also the most common cause of dizziness
worldwide, affecting an estimated 2.4% of the population over their lifetime and with an

secdrp__001_f.text:
annual incidence of 0.6%1.
For a UK population of 68,265,200 people12, a total of 409,591 people per year will be affected by BPPV.
Positional dizziness can also be caused by ‘structural’ central disorders, such as vertebrobasilar stroke and
tumours, or more commonly due to ‘non-structural’ causes such as vestibular migraine10. A neurological
examination may otherwise be normal, with the person only having position triggered nystagmus and
dizziness7. This leads to misdiagnosis and an assumption BPPV accounts for the symptoms and could have life-
threatening consequences. Therefore, improving awareness and early diagnosis of central positional dizziness is
critical to accessing the correct treatment.
How is BPPV diagnosed?
BPPV can affect any of the three semicircular canals in either inner ear, with the majority affecting the posterior
canal (85-95% of cases)8. There are five main subtypes of BPPV, each eliciting specific nystagmus features in the
tests3,8.
Diagnostic criteria for BPPV8 exist, emphasizing the importance of observing eye movements (nystagmus)
characteristics during canal-specific positioning manoeuvres, in order to make a diagnosis.
How many BPPV treatments are nee

secdrp__001_g.text:
ded, and does it recur?
Resolution rates for the most common subtype of BPPV are 72-74% after one treatment, increasing to 90% after
two treatments, depending on the manoeuvre used13. However, limited evidence exists regarding the optimal
number of treatments required for each of the five main BPPV subtypes3,8.
Indeed, in a survey I conducted in 2024 among 24 multidisciplinary professionals attending a UK dizziness
conference, there was no consensus on the appropriate number of treatments needed for resolution of the five
main subtypes of BPPV. This knowledge gap results in patients not receiving evidence-based care, and other
causes not being considered.
Additionally, BPPV is reported to recur at a rate of 36%13. While cardiovascular and metabolic risk factors have
proposed14,
been inconsistencies in findings and methodological issues limit the evidence.
In work package 2, I will study 250 participants (see sample size section) with positional dizziness, over 18
months to develop evidence on the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for the five main subtypes,
in order to guide clinical practice.
What are the challenges in diagnosing central causes?
Central causes are

secdrp__001_h.text:
suspected when atypical patterns of nystagmus are seen in the diagnostic manoeuvres10. Yet,
emerging research now suggests there may be atypical forms of BPPV that may account for these nystagmus
patterns15, complicating the process of diagnosis3,8, and increasing the risk that central causes may be overlooked
and not appropriately managed6.
Whilst specific nystagmus features may help identify central positional dizziness, at present central pathology is
often only considered when BPPV treatments fail10. However, given uncertainty over the number of
treatments required to resolve the five main subtypes of BPPV, relying on “failure to respond” may
contribute to missed or delayed diagnosis, ongoing symptoms, and exposes patients to unnecessary
treatment manoeuvres. Once a central cause such as vestibular migraine is identified, this can then be treated
appropriately16.
In work packages 1 and 2, I will review and expand on the published literature and identify the diagnostic
characteristics for central positional dizziness, to improve early detection and treatment of central causes.
Why do some patients not get better?
Effective treatments for BPPV have level 1 evidence supporting th

secdrp__001_i.text:
eir efficacy3,8. These manoeuvres aim to
reposition displaced debris from the affected semicircular canal by moving the patient’s head and body through
specific positions. Each position leverages gravity to clear debris from the canal, assuming a standard canal
orientation across all individuals.
Mathematical modelling indicates there are anatomical variations in canal geometry, which may explain why
approximately 10% of patients do not respond to treatment17. However, no imaging studies have investigated if
there is a variation that could account for treatment non-response.
This unexplored area represents a critical gap in our understanding of BPPV management and those that do not
respond. Thus, in work package 3, a subgroup of 50 participants (see sample size section) will have exploratory
3-dimensional models created from MRI scans of the inner ear. Identifying an anatomical variation could inform
future studies and refine the treatment manoeuvre positions to improve treatment outcome, reduce falls risk and
mortality4,5. These were research priorities agreed by the patient focus group, to improve the speed and accuracy
of diagnosis and treatment.
4. What are the research questio

secdrp__001_j.text:
n/aims and objectives?
Research aims:
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness (work package 1)
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness (work
package 2)
 Determine how many treatments are required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV (work
package 2)
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response (work package 3)
5. Project Plan
This fellowship will be formed of three interconnected work packages.
Work package 1: Systematic review
Objectives:
 Systematically review the nystagmus characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness
 Systematically review the patient risk factors and associated symptoms for central causes of positional
dizziness
 Explore what characteristics, risk factors and associated symptoms distinguish central from peripheral
causes of positional dizziness
In an existing systematic review on central positional dizziness18, only structural disorders were included, and the
included literature was restricted to three years. Non-str

secdrp__001_k.text:
uctural causes were omitted yet it is recognised that 90%
of people in a vestibular migraine attack could have positional dizziness10. Therefore, using PICO framework, I will
undertake a comprehensive and up-to-date systematic review that includes adults with positional dizziness (P),
confirmed by positional manoeuvres (I), compared to peripheral positional dizziness (C), to identify the diagnostic
features and associated symptoms for central positional dizziness (O).
I will register the systematic review with PROSPERO and meet with a UCL librarian to ensure a comprehensive
literature search is conducted of key databases.
The articles will be screened against predefined inclusion criteria in two stages, in collaboration with my research
supervisor (Professor Kaski). Initially, the titles and abstracts will be screened and then any excluded will have the
full text reviewed. Disagreements will be settled by a third reviewer, who is a member of the supervisory team with
expertise in undertaking systematic reviews (Dr Ellmers).
Included articles will be assessed for quality by two reviewers to reduce any bias, with a third reviewer involved if
there are any disagreements. Two reviewers

secdrp__001_l.text:
will extract relevant data using a preformatted data extraction form.
This will encompass the inclusion criteria (PICO) and specific details of the results. Risk of bias will be assessed
using ROBINS-E. The results will be reported using standardised guidelines, such as the PRISMA checklist. I will
seek support, as required, from my supervisory team who have experience in systematic reviews.
The diagnostic features identified from this systematic review will be embedded in the case record form used for
work package 2 and contribute towards addressing the second aim of this research fellowship.
Work package 2: Observational cohort study
Objectives:
 Characterise the nystagmus features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Establish accompanying symptoms and patient demographics that distinguish peripheral and central
causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required for each subtype of BPPV
 Ascertain the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
This work package will prospectively assess all patients presenting with dizziness that are triggered by head and
body position changes. All patients who present with BPPV will be treated,

secdrp__001_m.text:
and those that do not fulfil the
diagnostic criteria for BPPV or present with abnormalities in the eye examination referred for further review by a
specialist Neuro-otologist. It will expand on the systematic review to establish diagnostic differences between
peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness. It will also address critical gaps in the published literature on
the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV. The overall aim is to improve
accurate assessment and early treatment for patients with positional dizziness, whether that be due to peripheral
or central causes, priorities reported by the patient focus group.
Population
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, which includes white, Asian, and
Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London are Turkish, Irish, Polish, and Asian.
NCL is the second most deprived integrated care system in London19. These factors have been accounted for in
the development of this study, so that funding is available for participants travel and the provision of interpreters.
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The N

secdrp__001_n.text:
ational Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery. This
will ensure access to the specialist equipment (Rotundum chair) and acknowledges recommendations for future
research in positional dizziness10. The chair will allow a measurable (to 1 degree) and standardised position for
each patient undergoing the assessment and treatment manoeuvres, whilst simultaneously recording eye
movements20. These chairs are equally effective to bedside manaourvres for treatment 21 but allow more detailed
analysis of eye movements, to help identify the distinguishing features between peripheral and central causes of
positional dizziness and address this research fellowships aims. This will better inform bedside manoeuvres in the
majority of settings in which mechanical chairs are not available.
The study will be embedded within the Acute vertigo service, and Same Day Emergency Care service (SDEC), at
University College London Hospitals (UCLH) with oversight by a Consultant Neurologist (DK). This would be the
only location patients from NCL would receive acute neurology care within the emergency department.
This prospective study will be open for recruitment for 18 months. Purposive sampling from this di

secdrp__001_o.text:
verse population,
will allow all those who present with positional dizziness to access assessment and treatment, to address the
study aims. The patient advisory group will provide guidance during the study to maximise recruitment from
under-represented groups to ensure the results are generalisable. Figure 1 summarises the flow of each
participant through the study duration.
Figure 1: Participant flow through the study.
Sample size
Dizziness has a lifetime prevalence of 35%1. Among a population of 490 patients with dizziness, 20% were
reported to have dizziness triggered by position changes7, of which 80% attributed to BPPV and 20% to central
causes. To ensure sufficient statistical power, a prevalence rate of 20% was used to calculate the required
sample size for this study investigating positional dizziness.
The sample size was calculated with the support of statistical advisors, using the formula n=[(z)2 (p)(1-p)]/(d)2,
where z is 1.96 for 95% confidence, p (prevalence) is 0.20 and d (margin of error) is 0.0522. This resulted in a
required sample size of 250 participants to estimate the prevalence of positional nystagmus with a ±5% precision.
Recruitment feasibility is supported

secdrp__001_p.text:
by recent data from the UCLH acute vertigo service, where 240 patients with
dizziness (48 with positional dizziness) were seen in six months. Additional sources, including GP surgeries,
community teams, and self-referrals, make this target achievable over 18 months.
Participant recruitment
The study will be advertised through the NCL Primary Care network and community services for North Central
London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology teams in Barnet, Camden,
Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised in NCL emergency departments, NCL falls services and
internally at UCLH by email and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad recruitment strategy will ensure
the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness. These questions are adopted from a
diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of dizziness23, which was developed with patient involvement. Patients
guided the recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a telephone option on the
advertisements, both of which will be included.
Participants can be refe

secdrp__001_q.text:
rred by their GP or community therapy services from within North Central London. Internal
referrals from NCL emergency department (ED), NCL falls service, NCL hyper-acute stroke unit (HASU), and from
NCL therapy services will also be accepted. In addition, participants will be able to self-refer, to help reduce the
delays in diagnosis and treatment, two themes that were voiced as frustrations by the patient focus group. This
broad recruitment strategy will help increase recruitment of under-served groups.
Eligibility
Adults (over 18 years old) reporting dizziness, vertigo, or unsteadiness that is triggered by head or body
movements will be eligible to participate. Exclusion criteria include medical instability (cardiovascular, cervical, or
neurological), spontaneous nystagmus in sitting, pregnancy, and previous history of existing and persistent
vertigo. Consultee consent will be accepted for those unable to consent.
Screening and consent
Potential participants will be sent the participant information sheet electronically, as guided by the patient advisory
group. They will be given the opportunity to ask questions on the phone or in person, prior to providing consent.
Electronic or

secdrp__001_r.text:
paper-based consent will be obtained and stored in a central REDCap database set up for this
project. Participants will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment at the earliest point in time and their
GP informed of their participation.
Participant support
Potential participants can request the information sheet and consent form in paper format, as suggested by two
members of the patient focus group who did not have access to a computer. The signed paper copy of consent
will then be loaded into the secure database by the research team.
Potential participants who do not speak adequate English, will be able to either ask a family member to translate,
or request an interpreter is provided for them throughout this study. Access to an interpreter was included on
request of the patient focus group. Visual aids and a clear explanation of the manoeuvres and tests will be given
to each interpreter to translate.
To maximise participation of under-served groups24, participants will be able to select the time they visit the
hospital and travel costs will be remunerated promptly after each visit. Any non-attender will be contacted to
ensure any barriers to participation are mitiga

secdrp__001_s.text:
ted, with another appointment arranged if they wish to continue.
However, participants have the right to withdraw from the study at anytime without adversely affecting their
ongoing NHS care.
Data Management
Participants’ data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and scanning, but will be anonymised for any
dissemination. All data will be kept confidential and securely stored electronically, requiring a password to access
which will only be known by the research team. Data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine data
governance regulations.
Assessment visit
Participants will complete routine validated questionnaires used in dizziness and balance services (i.e. DHI, SVQ,
STAI-form Y, and VIMSSQ-short) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will help
identify any accompanying or distinguishing features of peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness.
Protected characteristics will also be collected to check for equality and inclusivity of those being recruited. These
will be reviewed monthly and discussed with the patient advisory group and The NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead, to help address any gaps in particip

secdrp__001_t.text:
ation. This may include adjusting how the study is
advertised or visiting specific clinics or teams in certain London boroughs.
Each assessment visit will take no more than one hour. It will include a lying and standing blood pressure check
for any postural hypotension, as the commonest non-vestibular cause of dizziness with position changes2 They
will then have a full eye movement examination in sitting using the video goggles. Positional tests3,8 using the
Rotundum chair will then be completed, whilst wearing the video goggles, to allow detailed analysis of the
nystagmus patterns to address the aims of work package 1 and 2.
criteria8,
Should BPPV be identified as per the diagnostic then the appropriate and established treatment
manoeuvre will be provided once, using the Rotundum chair. The treatments aim to relocate the displaced
otoconial debris from the affected semicircular canal, by moving the patient’s head and body through specific
positions. Each of these manoeuvres take no more than 3 minutes to complete.
Participants that do not fit the diagnostic criteria for the five main subtypes of BPPV in the positional tests8 or
present with abnormalities in the oculomotor examinat

secdrp__001_u.text:
ion, will be referred to the acute vertigo service for further
assessment and management by a specialist Neuro-otologist (Professor Kaski). This will end the participants
involvement in the study.
Follow up visits
To ensure the problem has fully resolved, participants will be followed up within two-weekly intervals, and re-
treated once per visit, if required. They will have a maximum of three follow up visits and treatments.
In this time, if no nystagmus is seen in the assessment manoeuvres, i.e. they no longer have BPPV, this will end
the participants involvement in the study.
Any participant that still has nystagmus, will be referred to the acute vertigo service for review by a Consultant
Neurologist. This is informed by the preparatory survey of specialists who reported that if BPPV did not resolve
after three treatments, they would investigate for other causes.
Recurrence
Any participant can contact the research team, at any point during the recruitment phase of the study, should they
feel their symptoms have recurred. This was requested at the patient focus group to speed up access to specialist
care. They will then follow the same intervention pathway as new participants (se

secdrp__001_v.text:
e Figure 1).
Statistical analysis
Descriptive analyses will summarise categorical data as frequencies and percentages, and continuous data as
means (±SD), or medians (IQR) based on distribution.
Regression models will be used to explore associations between clinical features and diagnoses of peripheral or
central positional nystagmus/dizziness. The primary outcome will be the diagnosis (peripheral vs. central causes).
Predictors include demographic variables, nystagmus features, and accompanying symptoms collected from
questionnaires. The regression analysis will adjust for covariates identified in WP1, and missing data will be
addressed using imputation methods. The supervisory team will ensure accurate statistical analysis.
The overall aim of this work package is to address priorities reported by the patient focus group and improve
accurate assessment and early treatment for patients with positional dizziness, whether that be due to peripheral
or central causes.
Work package 3: Pilot study
Objectives:
 Reconstruct 3D models of the inner ear from 3-tesla magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans
 Compare 3D models of the inner ear, of those that respond and do not respond to BPPV t

secdrp__001_w.text:
reatment, to
explore if there are any anatomical variations
 Recommend adjustments to the treatment manoeuvres for BPPV resolution
Method
This work package will use a subgroup of participants from work package 2 to explore if an anatomical difference
may account for why some patients with BPPV do not get better. There will be two groups of participants; those
that do not respond to three treatments, and those that do. Stratified purposive sampling will be used, with the
groups protected characteristics matched in order to ensure equality and inclusivity. Interpreters will be available,
and travel will be remunerated, as recommended by the patient focus group.
Participants will have high resolution MRI scans of the inner ear undertaken by the UCL imaging department. I will
collaborate closely with an academic radiologist (Dr Bray), leveraging his expertise to guide me in mastering the
skills to use specialist software to reconstruct 3D models of the inner ear from the scans, to enable comprehensive
visualisation and analysis of anatomy and fluid viscosity. Between group comparisons will be made to detect any
anatomical differences that could account for lack of resolution of BPPV.

secdrp__001_x.text:
Sample size
Among the 250 participants with positional dizziness in our study, it is anticipated that 10% (25 participants) will
not respond to the treatment manouevres17. This exploratory work package will compare these 25 non-
responders to an age- and gender-matched group of 25 responders from the same population. The exploratory
nature of this work aims to investigate potential anatomical differences between responders and non-responders.
Should anatomical differences be identified, the findings will inform future power calculations for larger studies to
investigate this more comprehensively. This knowledge may guide modifications to treatment protocols to improve
response rates in clinical practice.
Statistical analysis
High-resolution MRI scans will be used to create 3D reconstructions of the inner ear. Responders and non-
responders will be compared for differences in canal angle, orientation, width, and endolymph viscosity using
computational methods.
Statistical tests (e.g., t-tests) and multivariate models will adjust for age and gender to identify anatomical
predictors of treatment response. Findings will inform hypotheses and future research.
6. Dissemination, outputs,

secdrp__001_y.text:
and anticipated impact
Development of diagnostic criteria
Key diagnostic features for central positional dizziness identified in this project will be presented locally to the NCL
primary care network, and nationally to the British Society of Neuro-Otology, for consultation. The findings will
then be shared internationally to the Barany Society in order to propose diagnostic criteria for central positional
dizziness and refine treatment recommendations for BPPV that doctors and physiotherapists can implement.
Publications
I will submit the results to relevant peer reviewed journals for publication, anticipating at least three papers to
include:
 a systematic review to identify the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness
 describing the nystagmus and any key distinguishing features between peripheral and central positional
nystagmus
 presenting the findings from the first 3D reconstructive imaging study of the inner ear
Conferences
I will submit these results to specialist conferences attended by doctors and members of the multidisciplinary team,
including the renowned International Barany Society, European Academy of Neurologists, and the British

secdrp__001_z.text:
Society
of Neuro-Otology.
I will also share my results through the physiotherapy interest group (ACPIVR), at annual national or regional
training events and through their quarterly journal.
Multidisciplinary education
I will embed the findings in any education I provide through national and international courses, and through
university master’s programs. I will also offer to present the findings at any local departmental meetings.
Social media
I will share the results through a range of social media platforms, including the research unit at UCL, ACPIVR, and
my own personal accounts.
Patients and public
I have established a patient advisory group of eight members with experience of positional dizziness, aged 31 to
75 years, representing diverse ethnic backgrounds, 5 females and 3 males. They will guide how best to share the
findings to ensure the results are available for the people it affects and improves care in the future, for example:
 develop an infographic of the results.
 write layperson summaries for the professional routes of dissemination.
 present the results at a workshop for participants and members of the public to attend.
 write in the Ménière’s Disease society n

secdrp__001_{.text:
ewsletter.
Impact of the outputs:
The overall aim of this research fellowship is to simplify and improve the diagnosis and treatment of positional
dizziness, to improve patient care and quality of life, and reduce the burden on the NHS services.
Additionally, should an anatomical difference be identified in work package 3, this could inform future larger scale
research studies, leading to modifications to treatment manoeuvres and improved treatment outcome.
7. Project management
In order to successfully complete this research fellowship, I have put the following strategies in place:
I will hold weekly in-person meetings with my primary supervisor (Professor Kaski) to discuss progress, plan
actions, and address questions. Monthly in-person lab meetings at UCL with researchers and PhD students will
provide networking opportunities, peer support, and assistance with recruitment. Monthly online meetings with my
full supervisory team will ensure everyone is updated and allow for addressing questions, with additional individual
meetings as needed for work packages 1 and 3. Lastly, I will arrange quarterly online meetings with the patient
advisory group to refine participant resources, su

secdrp__001_|.text:
pport recruitment, and contribute to dissemination efforts.
8. Ethics
Once funding is approved, I will apply for internal ethical approval through the Joint Research Office at UCLH. I
will also complete the Integrated Research Application System (IRAS) form and apply for NHS ethical approval. In
order to reduce any delays, I will start these applications whilst waiting for funding approval.
9. Success criteria
Successful completion of this research fellowship will have the following criteria:
 Successfully meet recruitment target
 Determine the number of treatments for five main subtypes of BPPV
 Identify the recurrence rate per year, for each subtype of BPPV
 Confirm if an anatomical variation exists in those that do not respond to treatment
 Disseminate the findings (as per above)
 Attain a PhD
 Advance my clinical and research skills through completion of the proposed training and development plan
 Contribute to diagnostic criteria for central positional dizziness
To mitigate any risk of not achieving the recruitment target, I will monitor participant numbers each month using a
target vs. actual recruitment graph. I will collaborate closely with the patient advisory grou

secdrp__001_}.text:
p, NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead and my supervisory team to make timely adjustments to recruitment strategies,
ensuring that the target is met within the proposed timeframe.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Patients and the public have played a vital role in shaping this fellowship proposal. The study question and design
stem from recurring frustrations expressed by patients during routine clinical practice.
Subsequently, an online focus group of 10 patients, with a history of positional dizziness, shared their experiences.
Participants were from across the UK, due to University College London Hospital (UCLH) Trust’s tertiary service
reach, aged 26 to 78, representing diverse ethnicities, 6 females and 4 males. Six key themes emerged:
1. Delays in diagnosis – Patients often encounter delays, with dizziness in bed frequently misattributed to
BPPV without testing.
2. Challenges in treatment – A wide range of management approaches lead to inconsistent and sometimes
inadequate care.
3. Impact on quality of life – Positional dizziness causes significant frustration, anxiety, and disruption to
daily life.
4. Awareness gaps – Friends, family, and healthcare professionals often lack awareness that vestibular
migraine can mimic BPPV.
5. Communication barriers – Describing symptoms c

secppi__001_b.text:
an be difficult, especially for patients whose first
language is not English.
6. Access to care – Patients highlighted the need for improved access to specialist diagnosis and treatment,
suggesting self-referral pathways to expedite care.
These themes highlight the importance of the proposed study, to improve the diagnosis and treatment of positional
dizziness and improve patient quality of life. Consequent to this feedback, interpreters will be available, and
patients can self-refer for assessment during the recruitment period.
The focus group also reviewed the plain English summary of this proposal, recommending the aims were listed
after the background to the research section, for ease of readability. This was incorporated in the plain English
summary for this application.
Additionally, feedback from doctors managing BPPV in primary and emergency care has been instrumental.
During annual training events, they consistently report challenges due to limited time, insufficient training, and lack
of awareness about the varied causes of positional dizziness.
These insights underline the urgency of this research. This study aims to simplify the diagnosis of peripheral
versus central ca

secppi__001_c.text:
uses of positional dizziness, reduce delays and improve access to effective treatment, ultimately
enhancing patient outcomes and easing the burden on healthcare services.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
A patient advisory group, comprising eight members with experience of positional dizziness, aged 31 to 75
years, and representative of the study population (white, Asian, Black, and Turkish), was established through the
Ménière’s Disease Society, and will provide support throughout this study. Four online meetings a year will be
held, to reduce travel and time burdens. All members report access to an electronic device to take part and will be
remunerated for their time.
Roles and Contributions
1. Developing Resources
The group will review study advertisements and participant information sheets, ensuring they are accessible, free
from jargon, and culturally appropriate. They have a critical role to ensure potential participants understand the
study's purpose and feel comfortable engaging with it.
2. Recruitment
The group will help develop com

secppi__001_d.text:
munity-specific recruitment strategies to include wording in advertisements, to
maximise participation. This will occur prior to study commencement and at quarterly meetings when a summary
of the protected characteristics will be presented. They will help address gaps in participation, ensuring
underrepresented populations are included.
3. Dissemination
The group will collaborate to create dissemination materials to maximise the study’s impact. This includes
preparing plain English summaries for peer-reviewed journals, contributing to newsletters, developing an
infographic, social media posts, and public presentations.
Training and Support
To empower the advisory group to contribute effectively, training and support will be provided:
 Research design: Members will receive an overview of the study's objectives, methodologies, and
expected outcomes, ensuring they feel confident in their roles.
 Resource evaluation: Online training by the NIHR Learning for Involvement will cover best practices for
evaluating participant-facing materials, focusing on accessibility and inclusivity.
 Dissemination: Members will receive guidance from the NIHR Learning for Involvement, NIHR Evidence
and

secppi__001_e.text:
Plain English Campaign’s websites on creating impactful plain English summaries, preparing
presentation materials, and using digital platforms such as social media.
In addition, throughout the project support will be offered, including time to address questions or challenges.
Impact and reporting
The public involvement in research impact toolkit (PIRIT) will be completed in collaboration with the patient
advisory group. This will help to plan, track and evaluate public involvement.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
Proposed formal study
This PhD will be registered at UCL, and I will attend free workshops provided as part of their doctoral skills training
program. These include research governance and copyright, public engagement and impact, data visualisation,
thesis writing and advancing my presentation skills. These sessions will be distributed across the three-year
period, as outlined on the GANTT chart.
Academic training plan
To enhance these workshops, I have identified external courses that will help me to gain the skills required to
conduct the proposed research study (see also GANTT chart).
1. Gain skills in conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis
In order to complete work package 1, I will attend a four-day course “Introduction to systematic review and meta-
analysis” provided by the University of Bristol (£880.00). This course will teach the methodology of a systematic
review, how to perform a comprehensive literature review, how to evaluate the quality of a systematic review, and
how to summarise the findings of a systematic review and meta-analysis. I will also seek support from my
supervisory team, which includes a researcher w
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
ith experience in systematic reviews and meta-analyses (Dr
Ellmers).
2. Develop skills in Epidemiology
In work package 2, I will collect a wide range of data, from questionnaires and clinical assessment in order to build
predictive models. I will therefore attend a 15-day in-person course “Introductory Course in Epidemiology and
Medical Statistics”, provided by London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (£3700.00). This course will
teach me the concepts of epidemiology, statistical analysis and regression, data analysis and interpretation.
3. Develop skills in data analysis
I envisage collecting a large range of clinical data in work package 2, which I have not handled in previous
research. Therefore, I will attend the four-day course “Practical statistics for medical research” provided by UCL
(£750.00). This will provide the knowledge and skills to select, execute and interpret the required data analysis.
In addition, I will attend the two-day course “Data analytics with Python: Introduction” provided by City Lit
(£249.00). This will help me to learn how to use coding to organise, examine and analyse data, which will be a
useful skill when handling large datasets.
4. Gain skil
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
ls in assessing and treating positional dizziness
I will increase my experience in using a specialist chair (Rotundum chair) that standardises the diagnosis and
treatment of positional dizziness, alongside developing skills in interpreting the nystagmus collected by the video
goggles. These will be essential skills to develop for work package 2, as this will be the main data collection
method.
I have approached Dr Dan Dupont Hougaard, at Aalborg University Hospital in Denmark. He is an internationally
renowned ENT specialist with expertise in using mechanical repositioning chairs for over 10 years and is the most
published author on the topic. He has invited me to spend time within his clinic to gain skills in using the
Rotundum chair and in collecting and interpreting nystagmus recorded by video-nystagmography.
Following this overseas learning opportunity, I will join monthly clinics led by audiology and neurology colleagues
in the acute vertigo team at UCLH. This will provide additional opportunities to observe experts assessing patients
with positional dizziness and provide practice in interpreting the nystagmus captured by video-nystagmography. I
will collaborate with them to v
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
erify my analysis of the nystagmus recordings, enhancing my accuracy and ensuring
the data collection methods are robust.
5. 3D modelling
In order to complete work package 3, I need to learn skills in using specialist software to reconstruct 3D models to
make comparisons. Therefore, I will attend training provided by an academic radiologist (Dr Bray) and complete
online training to gain these skills. I will also collaborate with Dr Bray to verify my initial analyses are accurate.
6. Acquire skills to maximise knowledge translation
To make a meaningful difference to the management of positional dizziness, I need to ensure the findings from
this fellowship, and future studies I design as the next part of my NIHR academic pathway, are successfully
implemented. Therefore, towards the end of this fellowship, I will attend a six-week, part time course on
“Introduction to Implementation science” provided by Kings College London (£1400.00). This course will provide
skills to help with dissemination, engage stakeholders and design future studies.
Additionally, I wish to include patients and public in disseminating the research findings, to ensure they are
communicated effectively, in unders
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
tandable language and reach those that are affected. I will therefore attend a
free, four-week, part-time course on “Public Involvement in Research” provided by Imperial College London. This
course will ensure I maximise the skills of the patient advisory group and other collaborators for dissemination.
7. Develop confident skills in research leadership
The fellowship will be crucial to allow me to complete a PhD and progress my clinical academic career. I am keen
to build on my experience in clinical leadership, with a view to lead a team of researchers to continue investigating
positional dizziness and expand on the findings from this proposal. I will therefore attend the free “Resilient
Leadership in Action” course provided by UCL. This course is designed for early-stage career researchers and will
help me understand my leadership style, help to set up an effective research team and equip me with the skills to
lead that research team to maximise research impact.
Career development training
I will undertake this research fellowship full-time. Work package 2 will advance my skills in assessment and
treatment of the most common cause of dizziness and enhance my ability to triage an
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
d recognise central
disorders. These activities will be invaluable in pursuing my career as an academic researcher and developing
clinically relevant research proposals in the future.
Additionally, I will observe clinics once per month with the acute vertigo team. These activities will ensure breadth
to my clinical exposure and will also assist with patient recruitment.
I will also maintain my strong connections within special interest groups within physiotherapy (ACPIVR and
ACPIN) and the medical community (BSNO and Barany) through attendance at yearly conferences and quarterly
seminars.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Diego Kaski
SENSE Research Unit, Department of Clinical and Movement
Institution
Neurosciences, Institute of Neurology
Position Consultant Neurologist and Honorary Associate Professor
Description of support provided
Professor Diego Kaski is the Director of the SENSE Research Unit within the Department of Clinical and
Movement Neurosciences at the Institute of Neurology. His work focuses on the translational research to develop
novel therapies for patients with neurological balance disorders. His research programme aims to improve clinical
outcomes in
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
patients with vestibular and neurological balance disorders, focusing on novel clinical biomarkers to
monitor disease progression and treatment response, and design and implementation of treatment strategies.
He is one of the only Neurologists in the UK with expertise in central positional dizziness and is therefore
strategically placed to oversee this fellowship project. He will be providing research support focusing on study
design, recruitment strategy, research ethics, and ensuring successful completion of the study. He will also be
supporting the development of Amanda’s research, teaching and clinical skills, particularly with respect to career
satisfaction, career management and progression.
His background as a clinical-academic neurologist, successful research trajectory alongside his clinical expertise,
and established track record in working with numerous undergraduate and postgraduate students to completion
makes him an ideal supervisor for Amanda. He has overseen successful completion of 2 PhD students, and
currently has 4 PhD students as primary and 2 as secondary supervisor.
Professor Kaski holds monthly lab meetings, which Amanda will attend. He will also be holding 1
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
:1 semi-structured
meetings with the applicant throughout the Fellowship and will be available most days to discuss any concerns or
issues as required.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Gita Ramdharry
Institution National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery
Position Consultant Allied Health Professional in Neuromuscular Diseases
Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a Consultant Allied Health Professional specialising in neuromuscular diseases at the
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, UCLH, having begun her career as a clinical physiotherapist.
She has been affiliated with UCLH for over 20 years. She is the principal investigator of the Neuromuscular
Rehabilitation Research Group and has a special interest in balance in these cohorts.
Dr Ramdharry possesses significant research and statistics experience and holds academic appointments as an
Honorary Associate Professor at UCL. She has a strong understanding of the NIHR landscape, particularly the
Integrated Clinical and Practitioner Academic (ICA) programmes and is currently serving as a Deputy Chair of the
ICA Clinical Doctoral Awards panel. Her considerable supervisory experience includes m
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
entoring NIHR ICA
Clinical Doctoral Fellows and 5 PhD students to completion.
Dr Ramdharry’s inspiring combination of physiotherapy, NHS, academic, NIHR and mentoring experience put her
in an extremely strong position to support me in both my career development and in facilitating collaboration with
UCLH. Furthermore, I believe her extensive professional networks, which span neurology-focused and allied
health groups, along with her active involvement in national and international committees, may offer opportunities
to establish collaborations with a broad range of relevant professionals.
Of significance, Dr Ramdharry has a strong commitment to equality, diversity, inclusion (EDI), and patient
advocacy. Gaining her insights and guidance in these key areas will empower me to adopt best practices,
ensuring that my current, intended ACAF, and future research remains inclusive, equitable, and patient-centred.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Dr Toby Ellmers
Institution Department of Brain Sciences, Imperial College London
Position Sir Henry Wellcome Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Dr Toby Ellmers is a Sir Henry Wellcome Research Fellow in the Department of Brain Sci
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
ences, Imperial College
London, where he studies the neuro-cognitive factors contributing to dizziness and imbalance in older adults. In
2023, he received the Promising Scientist Award from the International Society of Posture & Gait Research for his
work.
Dr Ellmers is the co-lead of the ‘Concerns about Falling’ Working Group within the World Falls Guidelines global
initiative. This involved conducting a modified Delphi with ~100 of world experts within falls prevention and
management. As part of this process, Dr Ellmers led a systematic review and meta-analysis exploring if concerns
about falling predict future falls in older adults (manuscript in revision at Age and Ageing).
At Imperial College London, Dr Ellmers works alongside Professor Adolfo Bronstein and Dr Barry Seemungal,
eminent Consultant Neuro-Otologists and vestibular scientists. He has recently led the generation of clinical
guidelines for the assessment of positional dizziness in older adult fallers (https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afad206)
and a systematic review and meta-analysis that identified dizziness as a crucial risk factor for falls in older adults
(https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afae177).
Dr Ellmers has ext
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
ensive expertise in specialist statistical analysis and the research methodologies utilised in this
project (e.g., systematic reviews and meta-analyses, Delphi, large clinical datasets, etc.). He will provide hands-on
research training and support to the applicant on these methodologies throughout the Fellowship. He is currently
the primary supervisor for 3 PhD students and is secondary supervisor for 4 PhD students, 3 of whom are in their
final year.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 4
Name Dr Timothy Bray
Institution UCL Centre for Medical Imaging
Position Academic Consultant Radiologist
Description of support provided
Dr Timothy Bray is an Academic Consultant Radiologist based between the UCL Centre for Medical Imaging
(CMI), the UCL Hawkes Institute and the UCLH Imaging Department. He has an outstanding cross-disciplinary
skillset including both technical developments of novel MRI methods (with a recent patent submission) as well as
MRI translation and radiology. In total, he has over 40 publications including four publications in the top-ranked
MRI methodology journal and multiple top-ranked publications in MRI translation and radiology and has been
awarded >£3.5m grant funding fo
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
r his MRI research.
His unique, interdisciplinary skillset has been recognized through election to the International Society for Magnetic
Resonance in Medicine Junior Fellowship (2019) and Annual meeting Program Committee (2025-2028). He leads
the UCL/UCLH Integrated Academic Training Program in Radiology, and is Clinical Lead for the UCLH MRI
service,. He has supervised two PhD students to completion and currently supervises two more, as well as one
masters and one iBSc student.
He is Deputy Director of the newly-formed UCL Research Imaging Organisation (RIO), which brings together
world-leading infrastructure for imaging research at UCL under a single umbrella, and will help to oversee and
facilitate the developments proposed in this project.
Dr Bray has agreed to supervise this project, and meet regularly throughout the fellowship to support MRI
sequence setup and the development of a processing pipeline; this collaboration will also give me access to world-
leading experts in medical physics and statistics at CMI and RIO, providing an outstanding environment within
which to conduct my research.
Collaborations
International collaboration
This fellowship provides an opportunity t
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
o strengthen relationships with Dr Dan Dupont Hougaard, through an
overseas visit and through online meetings. We will continue to explore future research collaborations relating to
positional dizziness management, to build on findings from this fellowship and his current investigations. This will
allow a more varied population to be studied and increased generalisability of results for patient care
improvements.
North Central London (NCL) networking
I have developed a relationship with The NCL Community Neuroscience project lead, Dr Michelle Balaratnam, to
understand the impact of dizziness on patients in the emergency services, primary care and community services
across NCL. Their neuroscience network will be crucial for advertising the study broadly and ensuring
underserved groups are included. I will also build further relationships and visit surgeries and other clinical
services to promote the study, ensure equality and inclusivity in participation, and also to disseminate the findings.
Meniere’s Society (UK balance and dizziness Charity)
I have established associations with the Meniere’s Society and have worked alongside the Director as a member
of the Balance Interest Group.
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
More recently I have published in their magazine and recruited members of the
patient advisory group through them. I will continue to develop this relationship for the dissemination streams
through presentations, newsletters and their balance awareness week events upon completion.
Physiotherapy networks (ACPIVR)
I was one of the founding members of this growing special interest group, which now has over 1200 members.
Despite completing my term, I remain passionate about the aims of the group and frequently provide education for
members. I will use these links to ensure the results are disseminated through their local and national
conferences, workshops and bi-annual journal.
UCL relationships and support
This fellowship will be conducted out of the SENSE research unit at UCL, comprised of distinguished clinical
researchers, Masters and PhD students and research fellows from across the globe. I will join monthly department
meetings overseen by the unit’s director (DK) and also meet with other PhD students registered at UCL. This will
create a rich environment for peer support, collaboration, facilitate an international network through which to
disseminate findings and help to forge
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
future collaborations.
In addition to completing the above mentioned statistics training, I will continue to collaborate with the Medical
Statistics team at UCL and members of my supervisory team with statistical expertise (GR and TE), to ensure my
data analysis is thorough and accurate.
Commercial relationships
This research fellowship will use a specialist repositioning chair to diagnose causes of positional dizziness and
treat benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), which was donated as part of a philanthropic gesture to the
research unit. I have established a good relationship with Norbert Wick, the managing director of Balcare, who
installed the Rotundum rotatory chair and provided basic training. He will support the project with maintenance,
