repairs, and technical assistance. There are no conflicts of interest or funding associated with this collaboration.
Interdisciplinary networks
I have an established association with the lead Occupational Therapist (Vassilios Tahtis) at King’s College
Hospital, London. I trained all of the occupational therapists and physiotherapists in the department, to help them
identify vestibular problems. We also published a paper together to
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
improve training in positional manoeuvres for
BPPV. He is keen to help disseminate the findings of this research.
Additionally, I have been contacted by an ENT specialist in Athens (Dr George Korres) to discuss current clinical
challenges with positional dizziness. We have identified several common areas, and plan to write a manuscript for
publication, that will synthesise the literature on positional nystagmus and dizziness so far.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
As the Head of the Clinical and Movement Neurosciences Department at University College London, I wish to
express my unreserved support for Ms Amanda Male’s NIHR doctoral fellowship application.
The Department of Clinical and Movement Neurosciences is a world-leading group that brings together expertise
across the clinical and basic science spectrum of the physiology and pathology of human movement and
movement disorders. Ms Male will benefit from being base
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
d in the SENSE Research Unit, which is a unique group
pursuing research in translating behavioural neuroscience into experimental medicine and treatments for adults
with vestibular and balance disorders. Ms Male’s training as a physiotherapist and research in physiotherapy
approaches to manage dizziness perfectly aligns with the multidisciplinary structure of this department.
As the host organisation, we are committed to providing the necessary infrastructure and support for the
successful completion of this fellowship. We recognise the importance of this fellowship in facilitating the
development of highly skilled clinical academic researchers who will make significant and direct contributions to
improving patient care. We value the importance of creating a research environment that is inclusive, as evidenced
by the range of subspecialty lab members, gender, and ethnicity within the team. We strive to ensure we provide a
supportive environment for all our lab members and collaborators, holding weekly meetings to discuss general lab
matters, offering an opportunity to evaluate research integrity, and offering 1-1 meetings with those that need
additional support. We expect and suppo
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
rt all new members to abide by the UCL principles of equality, diversity,
and inclusion and ensure that members receive appropriate training in this regard, prior to starting.
We will ensure that Ms Male is provided with adequate resources, including access to facilities, equipment, and
training, to enable her to carry out her research project effectively. She will have access to world-leading facilities
and technology in the gait laboratory at 33 Queen Square together, most notably the Rotundum chair that is
integral to her project, with the highest quality training. In addition, we will provide Ms Male with doctoral
mentorship and steer from an internationally recognised clinical academic, Associate Prof Diego Kaski, an expert
in behavioural neurology and vestibular disorders. The Department also benefits from direct links with experienced
researchers and clinicians at Queen Square and Royal Free hospitals to support her research. Ms Male shows
every sign of being at the forefront of her field and we are keen for her to join our department.
The department has an agreed pathway for the development of clinical academic posts at pre- and post-doctoral
level and in the medium term we
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
will help her conceptualise and deliver a top-quality NIHR post-doctoral fellowship
application to ensure she continues on a clinical academic path in the longer term.
As the host organisation, we are committed to ensuring Ms Male’s success as a clinical academic researcher
throughout her fellowship and beyond. We believe that this fellowship will significantly contribute to the
advancement of healthcare and the development of a highly qualified clinical academic researcher.
Yours sincerely,
Professor Tony Schapira
Head of Department of Clinical and Movement Neurosciences
UCL Queen Square Institute of Neurology and National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery
Queen Square
London WC1N 3BG

implications: Applicant CV: Lists MRes, BSc, and specialized vestibular courses, demonstrating formal training and relevant clinical experience.
Applicant Research Background: Details extensive experience in study design, data analysis, and mixed-methods research, directly relevant to the proposed doctoral work.
Applicant CV: Extensive publication record (2018-2024) in neurology and otology journals, plus a research grant, indicating strong research output.
Applicant Research Background: Lists multiple peer-reviewed publications in high-impact journals and a secured research grant, demonstrating strong research output.
Applicant CV: Progression from Junior to Senior Physiotherapist and Honorary Research Associate shows increasing responsibility and leadership.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates leadership through service development, committee chairmanship, mentoring students, and co-editing journal supplements.
Applicant CV: Current role as Vestibular Physiotherapist alongside research publications demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic career path.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly states the goal of becoming a clinical academic leader and details the integration of clinical practice with research activities.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a vision to become a clinical academic leader and outlines a trajectory from clinical practice to a PhD fellowship.
Applicant Research Background: Explains the need for the award to secure dedicated time for research, which was previously unavailable in full-time NHS roles, enabling career progression.
Applicant Research Background: Highlights potential to enhance patient quality of life and alleviate NHS strain, supported by positive feedback from a charity regarding patient benefit.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights impact on patient quality of life, falls risk, and clinician guidelines, clearly articulating potential benefits.
Detailed Research Plan: Articulates clear benefits including reduced falls risk, improved diagnostic accuracy, and reduced NHS strain, directly supporting pr.5.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement and Plain English Summary, providing structural evidence for impact assessment.
Applicant Research Background: Provides a clear total cost breakdown and details existing funding from the Meniere's Society, justifying the remaining resource request.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed justifications linking specific costs (salary, MRI, questionnaires) to research tasks and work packages, supporting pr.7.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of SUMMARY BUDGET with 140 table lines and high lexical overlap with Research Plan, supporting resource justification checks.
Applicant Research Background: Mentions establishing a patient advisory group and conducting patient focus groups, indicating integration of lived experience.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly states patient feedback shaped the research question and study design, supporting wp.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Demonstrates integration of lived experience through patient focus groups influencing study design, recruitment materials, and dissemination formats, supporting wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details how patient focus groups shaped the study question, design, and plain English summary, with specific changes incorporated based on feedback.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for lived experience integration.
Applicant Research Background: Outlines plans for dissemination via charity newsletters, awareness events, and peer-reviewed publications to maximize public benefit.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines dissemination to clinicians and patients via journals, conferences, social media, and workshops, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Details a comprehensive dissemination plan including peer-reviewed papers, conferences, and patient-facing infographics to maximize public benefit, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines a comprehensive dissemination plan involving the patient advisory group in creating plain English summaries, infographics, and social media posts.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for public benefit maximization.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates the problem, aims, methods, and beneficiaries in accessible language, supporting pr.1.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show strong readability (Flesch-Kincaid 7.59, 0% long sentences) and low jargon density (3.58%), with coverage of all required summary components, supporting pr.1.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Plain English Summary and NLP analysis sections, providing structural evidence for readability and content coverage checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies specific knowledge gaps regarding brain causes, treatment frequency, and non-responders, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly identifies knowledge gaps regarding central causes, treatment recurrence, and anatomical variations, supporting pr.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly identifies critical gaps in literature regarding treatment frequency, recurrence rates, and anatomical variations causing non-response, supporting pr.2.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan and Scientific Abstract, providing structural evidence for evidence review and gap identification checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines three distinct studies with defined sample sizes (250 and 25), indicating feasible scope, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines three distinct studies with specific sample sizes (250 and 25), indicating a feasible scope aligned with award expectations.
Detailed Research Plan: Defines three work packages with specific sample sizes (250 and 25) and an 18-month timeline, demonstrating feasible scope aligned with award expectations.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan and SUMMARY BUDGET, providing structural evidence for scope and feasibility checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific methods including literature review, questionnaires, assessments, and 3D inner ear scans, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Details specific methods including systematic review, video-goggle assessments, and 3D MRI reconstruction, demonstrating methodological rigor.
Detailed Research Plan: Details rigorous methods including PICO framework for systematic review, Rotundum chair for standardized assessment, and 3D MRI reconstruction, supporting pr.4.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan with high word count, providing structural evidence for methodological rigor checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details patient involvement in shaping study design, advertising, and recruitment, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions patient focus groups informing challenges and plans for patient workshops, indicating engagement with people and communities.
Detailed Research Plan: Describes active involvement of a patient advisory group in shaping recruitment, consent processes, and dissemination strategies, supporting pr.6.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for engagement checks.
Plain English Summary of Research: Mentions providing interpreters and refunding travel costs to ensure inclusive participation, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions recruiting adults in North Central London, though specific inclusive strategies are less detailed here than in the Plain English Summary.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines specific inclusive strategies such as interpreter provision, travel cost remuneration, and flexible scheduling to support under-served groups, supporting pr.9.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates specific funds for travel reimbursement and interpreters to ensure equality and inclusivity for diverse participants, supporting pr.9.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan and Patient & Public Involvement, providing structural evidence for inclusive design checks.
Detailed Research Plan: Targets a diverse population in North Central London with specific strategies for ethnic minorities and non-English speakers, supporting wp.4.

Patient & Public Involvement: Highlights diverse representation in the advisory group (ethnicities, ages) and specific strategies to address communication barriers and include underrepresented populations.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for representation of underserved groups.
Detailed Research Plan: Includes plans for monthly review of protected characteristics and adaptive recruitment strategies to ensure inclusivity across the lifecycle, supporting wp.5.

Patient & Public Involvement: Describes involvement across the lifecycle from design and recruitment to dissemination, with quarterly reviews of protected characteristics to ensure ongoing inclusivity.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for inclusivity across the lifecycle.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly allocates resources for interpreters and travel costs to support inclusion, indicating a costed plan for research inclusion, supporting wp.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Confirms members will be remunerated and provided with training (NIHR Learning for Involvement), indicating a resourced plan for inclusion.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes costed lines for patient advisory group remuneration, workshops, and dissemination materials, demonstrating a resourced plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement and SUMMARY BUDGET with high lexical overlap, supporting costed inclusion plan checks.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details a comprehensive training plan including UCL workshops, external courses in systematic reviews, epidemiology, and data analysis, directly supporting td.1.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details specific training courses (systematic review, statistics, Python, implementation science) with costs and justifications linking them to project needs, supporting td.1.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section with high word count, providing structural evidence for training quality checks.

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly identifies skill gaps (e.g., handling large clinical datasets, 3D modelling) and maps specific courses and mentorship to address them, supporting td.2.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Maps training activities (e.g., data analytics, overseas visits for specialist equipment) directly to skill gaps required for work packages 2 and 3, supporting td.2.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section with high lexical overlap to Research Plan, suggesting alignment with project needs.

Training & Development and Research Support: Links training in implementation science and leadership to career goals of becoming a clinical academic leader and securing future fellowships, supporting td.3.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Links training in implementation science and epidemiology to the applicant's NIHR academic pathway and future career goals, supporting td.3.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for career aspiration support checks.
Training & Development and Research Support: Highlights supervisors' strong track records, including PhD completions, significant grant funding, and high-impact publications, supporting ss.1.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for organizational track record checks.
Training & Development and Research Support: Describes structured supervision with monthly meetings, 1:1 sessions, and specific mentorship on study design and statistics, supporting ss.2.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for supervisory support checks.

Training & Development and Research Support: Host organisation confirms access to world-leading facilities (Rotundum chair, gait lab) and a clear pathway for post-doctoral progression, supporting ss.3.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for organizational appropriateness checks.
Training & Development and Research Support: Host statement and supervisor profiles emphasize commitment to EDI, inclusive culture, and research integrity training, supporting ss.4.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for inclusive culture checks.
Training & Development and Research Support: Host statement notes the diversity of the SENSE Research Unit team across gender and ethnicity, supporting wp.2.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Training & Development section, providing structural evidence for team diversity checks.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision to become a clinical academic leader (g.1.a) with a distinctive focus on vestibular disorders and implementation science (g.1.b). The training plan is exceptionally well-justified, linking specific courses to work packages and career goals (g.1.c). Strong mentorship is evidenced by detailed supervisor profiles and a structured supervision framework (g.1.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'independent direction' (g.1.b) is heavily reliant on the established expertise of the supervisors, though this is typical for a fellowship application.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'independent direction' (g.1.b) is heavily reliant on the established expertise of the supervisors, though this is typical for a fellowship application.

Please describe your research career to date
I am recognised as one of the clinical leaders in the field of vestibular physiotherapy in the United Kingdom. I
believe I have the experience, ambition and motivation to successfully complete this doctoral fellowship and
become a future clinical academic leader in the specialty.
Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute
stroke population (January 2009–January 2011). This quantitative study, involving five patients, was presented as
a poster at the World Confederation for Physiotherapy (June 2011). The experience provided valuable insights into
the research process and sparked my passion for integrating research with clinical practice.
I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

tibular physiotherapists. My
strong relationships within the multidisciplinary clinical and research community have been instrumental in
advancing patient care and contributing to impactful research.
Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application,

o develop this fellowship
application to advance my academic career.
In addition to my professional ambitions, I have qualified for Great Britain’s Age Group Triathlon teams, for both
the European Championships (sprint distance) and World Championships (Olympic distance) in 2025. The last
competition takes place in Australia in October 2025, and this is why I have deferred the start date of this research
fellowship until January 2026 to allow these competitions to have concluded. I have the support of my supervisory
team, to ensure I will be able to manage this research fellowship in future, alongside the training for triathlon
events, so that I can pursue both my professional and sporting ambitions.

patients with vestibular and neurological balance disorders, focusing on novel clinical biomarkers to
monitor disease progression and treatment response, and design and implementation of treatment strategies.
He is one of the only Neurologists in the UK with expertise in central positional dizziness and is therefore
strategically placed to oversee this fellowship project. He will be providing research support focusing on study
design, recruitment strategy, research ethics, and ensuring successful completion of the study. He will also be
supporting the development of Amanda’s research, teaching and clinical skills, particularly with respect to career
satisfaction, career management and progression.
His background as a clinical-academic neurologist, successful research trajectory alongside his clinical expertise,
and established track record in working with numerous undergraduate and postgraduate students to completion
makes him an ideal supervisor for Amanda. He has overseen successful completion of 2 PhD students, and
currently has 4 PhD students as primary and 2 as secondary supervisor.

Name Dr Gita Ramdharry

Position Consultant Allied Health Professional in Neuromuscular Diseases
Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a Consultant Allied Health Professional specialising in neuromuscular diseases at the
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, UCLH, having begun her career as a clinical physiotherapist.
She has been affiliated with UCLH for over 20 years. She is the principal investigator of the Neuromuscular
Rehabilitation Research Group and has a special interest in balance in these cohorts.
Dr Ramdharry possesses significant research and statistics experience and holds academic appointments as an
Honorary Associate Professor at UCL. She has a strong understanding of the NIHR landscape, particularly the
Integrated Clinical and Practitioner Academic (ICA) programmes and is currently serving as a Deputy Chair of the
ICA Clinical Doctoral Awards panel. Her considerable supervisory experience includes m

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly articulates why this award is the right next step, noting the inability to conduct dedicated research while in full-time NHS roles (g.2.a, g.2.b). The award is positioned to build on existing clinical experience by adding specific research skills like systematic reviews and data analytics (g.2.c). Partnerships with the Meniere's Society and NHS trusts are highlighted as key to developing research networks (g.2.d).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: Evidence for developing partnerships with industry or social care organizations beyond the NHS and charity sector is less explicit.
sub_criteria.rationale: Evidence for developing partnerships with industry or social care organizations beyond the NHS and charity sector is less explicit.

● Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding or fewer publications. In general terms,
contextual factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that the information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with the
General Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).
The last NHS position I held had no research time apportioned and the data collection phase of the observational
study we had received charity funding (Lily Foundation) for, had finished. There was a large amount of data that
required

analysing, findings to share with the patients and public, and professional manuscripts to be written.
I am committed to progressing my career as a clinical academic researcher, and my obligations to disseminate
results from my research activities. As such, I made the decision to leave my full-time NHS position to allow time
to focus on my research and took up a part time, honorary (unpaid) contract role based in the SENSE research
unit, at UCL. This part time role has allowed the time to prepare and successfully publish three articles relating to
the Lily Foundation research grant and assist in one other. It also allowed time to publish one more article relating
to this fellowship topic and be an editor for a standalone supplement in the Journal of laryngology and otology.
Moreover, alongside the research team, I continue to analyse the data to address secondary variables.
In addition, this part time role in the SENSE research unit has provided a rich opportunity to collaborate and
network with current UCL PhD students and other distinguished researchers. It has provided time to undertake
the patient focus group, establish a patient advisory group for this project, and the time t

tandable language and reach those that are affected. I will therefore attend a
free, four-week, part-time course on “Public Involvement in Research” provided by Imperial College London. This
course will ensure I maximise the skills of the patient advisory group and other collaborators for dissemination.
7. Develop confident skills in research leadership
The fellowship will be crucial to allow me to complete a PhD and progress my clinical academic career. I am keen
to build on my experience in clinical leadership, with a view to lead a team of researchers to continue investigating
positional dizziness and expand on the findings from this proposal. I will therefore attend the free “Resilient
Leadership in Action” course provided by UCL. This course is designed for early-stage career researchers and will
help me understand my leadership style, help to set up an effective research team and equip me with the skills to
lead that research team to maximise research impact.
Career development training
I will undertake this research fellowship full-time. Work package 2 will advance my skills in assessment and
treatment of the most common cause of dizziness and enhance my ability to triage an

disorders. These activities will be invaluable in pursuing my career as an academic researcher and developing

Additionally, I will observe clinics once per month with the acute vertigo team. These activities will ensure breadth
to my clinical exposure and will also assist with patient recruitment.
I will also maintain my strong connections within special interest groups within physiotherapy (ACPIVR and

Please describe your research career to date
I am recognised as one of the clinical leaders in the field of vestibular physiotherapy in the United Kingdom. I
believe I have the experience, ambition and motivation to successfully complete this doctoral fellowship and
become a future clinical academic leader in the specialty.
Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute
stroke population (January 2009–January 2011). This quantitative study, involving five patients, was presented as
a poster at the World Confederation for Physiotherapy (June 2011). The experience provided valuable insights into
the research process and sparked my passion for integrating research with clinical practice.
I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

, analysing, and disseminating
research using quantitative and qualitative methodologies. My dissertation involved a mixed-methods survey of
physiotherapists’ management of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) in the UK, which required university
ethics approval. The findings were published in the Journal of Physiotherapy (September 2019) and presented as
posters at the European Region of World Confederation for Physiotherapy (November 2021).
Returning to my NHS role, I co-led a project identifying vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease patients, in
the largest cohort studied to date. This research was published in the Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and
Psychiatry (November 2018) and presented at the Bárány Society Meeting (June 2018). Building on these results,
we secured funding from The Lily Foundation (May 2019) for a prospective observational study. I co-developed a
diagnostic framework for vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease, supervised research associates,
managed data, and adapted the methodology during the COVID-19 pandemic. I created an electronic case record
form and coordinated research activities within the CPMS. I conducted a modified D

elphi consensus for the
framework development, published in Journal of Neurology (June 2023) and shared as a poster at the Bárány
Society Meeting (May 2022). I led the dissemination of the framework’s sensitivity and specificity results in the
European Journal of Neurology (May 2024) and published a patient survey analysis in Audiology Research (June
2024).
In collaboration with the acute vertigo service, I co-authored a publication highlighting the prevalence of central
versus peripheral causes of positional vertigo and common diagnostic errors by non-specialists. These findings
were presented at the British Society of Neuro-Otology annual conference (October 2023).
My research endeavours have fuelled my interest in positional dizziness and equipped me with robust skills in
designing, conducting, and disseminating studies. Beyond my projects, I have peer-reviewed manuscripts for
various journals and co-edited a specialist supplement on dizziness and balance disorders for the Journal of
Laryngology and Otology (May 2024). I also co-authored a British Society of Audiology practice guideline on
vestibular rehabilitation (April 2019) and contributed two case studies to the book “Puzzl

My research endeavours have fuelled my interest in positional dizziness and equipped me with robust skills in

ing Cases in Vertigo and
Dizziness”.
Clinical and leadership experience
I have worked clinically as a vestibular and neurological physiotherapist since 2002, and have developed and led
three vestibular rehabilitation services, within secondary and tertiary NHS services and the private sector. I have
developed advanced clinical skills in vestibular assessment and treatment, through mentoring by medical and
vestibular specialists, independent professional development, and attendance at specialist courses in the UK and
overseas.
I was responsible for the supervision of junior colleagues, appraisals, and performance management. I have
supported team members to analyse data, present audits, develop service improvement ideas and report the
findings to managers. I have helped others design posters for conferences and pass these skills onto more junior
team members. In my last position, I supported the entire therapy outpatient department in a major transformation
project to adopt an online documentation system and coordinated two department relocations and changes in
service delivery.
My interest in vestibular rehabilitation as a speciality, led me to become one of the founding members of

the
Chartered Society of Physiotherapy special interest group – the Association of Chartered Physiotherapist
interested in Vestibular Rehabilitation (ACPIVR). I was a committee member for eight years (2008-2016),
culminating in 2 years as vice chairperson, and the final two years as chairperson. This committee was
responsible for developing the knowledge and skills of other physiotherapists nationally, raising awareness of the
specialism, and developing resources for clinicians and patients.
I am passionate about providing evidence-based treatment and support others to do the same. I frequently
provide training locally, nationally and internationally, to physiotherapists and a variety of medical professionals on
vestibular rehabilitation and BPPV management. I lecture on several University College London (UCL) Master’s
programs and have supervised three Master’s student’s research projects, to include supporting one to publish
their findings in a peer reviewed journal.
Impact of Fellowship
I am privileged to have an honorary position with one of the world’s leading neuro-otology research units at UCL, a
testament to my standing as one of the UK’s most experienced and respected ves

I have worked clinically as a vestibular and neurological physiotherapist since 2002, and have developed and led

tibular physiotherapists. My

Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide

feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.

criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'independent direction' (g.1.b) is heavily reliant on the established expertise of the supervisors, though this is typical for a fellowship application.

Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute

I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

tibular physiotherapists. My
strong relationships within the multidisciplinary clinical and research community have been instrumental in

Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.

The last NHS position I held had no research time apportioned and the data collection phase of the observational
study we had received charity funding (Lily Foundation) for, had finished. There was a large amount of data that
required

analysing, findings to share with the patients and public, and professional manuscripts to be written.
I am committed to progressing my career as a clinical academic researcher, and my obligations to disseminate
results from my research activities. As such, I made the decision to leave my full-time NHS position to allow time
to focus on my research and took up a part time, honorary (unpaid) contract role based in the SENSE research
unit, at UCL. This part time role has allowed the time to prepare and successfully publish three articles relating to
the Lily Foundation research grant and assist in one other. It also allowed time to publish one more article relating
to this fellowship topic and be an editor for a standalone supplement in the Journal of laryngology and otology.
Moreover, alongside the research team, I continue to analyse the data to address secondary variables.
In addition, this part time role in the SENSE research unit has provided a rich opportunity to collaborate and
network with current UCL PhD students and other distinguished researchers. It has provided time to undertake
the patient focus group, establish a patient advisory group for this project, and the time t

tandable language and reach those that are affected. I will therefore attend a
free, four-week, part-time course on “Public Involvement in Research” provided by Imperial College London. This
course will ensure I maximise the skills of the patient advisory group and other collaborators for dissemination.
7. Develop confident skills in research leadership
The fellowship will be crucial to allow me to complete a PhD and progress my clinical academic career. I am keen
to build on my experience in clinical leadership, with a view to lead a team of researchers to continue investigating
positional dizziness and expand on the findings from this proposal. I will therefore attend the free “Resilient
Leadership in Action” course provided by UCL. This course is designed for early-stage career researchers and will
help me understand my leadership style, help to set up an effective research team and equip me with the skills to
lead that research team to maximise research impact.
Career development training
I will undertake this research fellowship full-time. Work package 2 will advance my skills in assessment and
treatment of the most common cause of dizziness and enhance my ability to triage an

Please describe your research career to date
I am recognised as one of the clinical leaders in the field of vestibular physiotherapy in the United Kingdom. I
believe I have the experience, ambition and motivation to successfully complete this doctoral fellowship and
become a future clinical academic leader in the specialty.
Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute
stroke population (January 2009–January 2011). This quantitative study, involving five patients, was presented as
a poster at the World Confederation for Physiotherapy (June 2011). The experience provided valuable insights into
the research process and sparked my passion for integrating research with clinical practice.
I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

, analysing, and disseminating
research using quantitative and qualitative methodologies. My dissertation involved a mixed-methods survey of
physiotherapists’ management of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) in the UK, which required university
ethics approval. The findings were published in the Journal of Physiotherapy (September 2019) and presented as
posters at the European Region of World Confederation for Physiotherapy (November 2021).
Returning to my NHS role, I co-led a project identifying vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease patients, in
the largest cohort studied to date. This research was published in the Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and
Psychiatry (November 2018) and presented at the Bárány Society Meeting (June 2018). Building on these results,
we secured funding from The Lily Foundation (May 2019) for a prospective observational study. I co-developed a
diagnostic framework for vestibular dysfunction in mitochondrial disease, supervised research associates,
managed data, and adapted the methodology during the COVID-19 pandemic. I created an electronic case record
form and coordinated research activities within the CPMS. I conducted a modified D

elphi consensus for the
framework development, published in Journal of Neurology (June 2023) and shared as a poster at the Bárány
Society Meeting (May 2022). I led the dissemination of the framework’s sensitivity and specificity results in the
European Journal of Neurology (May 2024) and published a patient survey analysis in Audiology Research (June
2024).
In collaboration with the acute vertigo service, I co-authored a publication highlighting the prevalence of central
versus peripheral causes of positional vertigo and common diagnostic errors by non-specialists. These findings
were presented at the British Society of Neuro-Otology annual conference (October 2023).
My research endeavours have fuelled my interest in positional dizziness and equipped me with robust skills in
designing, conducting, and disseminating studies. Beyond my projects, I have peer-reviewed manuscripts for
various journals and co-edited a specialist supplement on dizziness and balance disorders for the Journal of
Laryngology and Otology (May 2024). I also co-authored a British Society of Audiology practice guideline on
vestibular rehabilitation (April 2019) and contributed two case studies to the book “Puzzl

Participation in professional development programs and co-editing journal supplements suggests leadership potential (g.5.c, g.5.d).
criteria.drawbacks: While leadership potential is evident, the applicant is at an early stage of their independent research career, which is appropriate for this fellowship level.

In collaboration with the acute vertigo service, I co-authored a publication highlighting the prevalence of central

My research endeavours have fuelled my interest in positional dizziness and equipped me with robust skills in
designing, conducting, and disseminating studies. Beyond my projects, I have peer-reviewed manuscripts for

ing Cases in Vertigo and
Dizziness”.
Clinical and leadership experience
I have worked clinically as a vestibular and neurological physiotherapist since 2002, and have developed and led
three vestibular rehabilitation services, within secondary and tertiary NHS services and the private sector. I have
developed advanced clinical skills in vestibular assessment and treatment, through mentoring by medical and
vestibular specialists, independent professional development, and attendance at specialist courses in the UK and
overseas.
I was responsible for the supervision of junior colleagues, appraisals, and performance management. I have
supported team members to analyse data, present audits, develop service improvement ideas and report the
findings to managers. I have helped others design posters for conferences and pass these skills onto more junior
team members. In my last position, I supported the entire therapy outpatient department in a major transformation
project to adopt an online documentation system and coordinated two department relocations and changes in
service delivery.
My interest in vestibular rehabilitation as a speciality, led me to become one of the founding members of

the
Chartered Society of Physiotherapy special interest group – the Association of Chartered Physiotherapist
interested in Vestibular Rehabilitation (ACPIVR). I was a committee member for eight years (2008-2016),
culminating in 2 years as vice chairperson, and the final two years as chairperson. This committee was
responsible for developing the knowledge and skills of other physiotherapists nationally, raising awareness of the
specialism, and developing resources for clinicians and patients.
I am passionate about providing evidence-based treatment and support others to do the same. I frequently
provide training locally, nationally and internationally, to physiotherapists and a variety of medical professionals on
vestibular rehabilitation and BPPV management. I lecture on several University College London (UCL) Master’s
programs and have supervised three Master’s student’s research projects, to include supporting one to publish
their findings in a peer reviewed journal.

Please describe your research career to date
I am recognised as one of the clinical leaders in the field of vestibular physiotherapy in the United Kingdom. I
believe I have the experience, ambition and motivation to successfully complete this doctoral fellowship and
become a future clinical academic leader in the specialty.
Research experience
I began my research journey during a clinical physiotherapy position, where I collaborated with a colleague to
conceptualise, design, and conduct a pilot study on a novel intervention for upper limb function in the sub-acute
stroke population (January 2009–January 2011). This quantitative study, involving five patients, was presented as
a poster at the World Confederation for Physiotherapy (June 2011). The experience provided valuable insights into
the research process and sparked my passion for integrating research with clinical practice.
I pursued a full-time Master’s of Research in Clinical Practice at St. George’s University of London (September
2015), funded by the NIHR. This program honed my skills in designing, executing

ing Cases in Vertigo and
Dizziness”.
Clinical and leadership experience
I have worked clinically as a vestibular and neurological physiotherapist since 2002, and have developed and led
three vestibular rehabilitation services, within secondary and tertiary NHS services and the private sector. I have
developed advanced clinical skills in vestibular assessment and treatment, through mentoring by medical and
vestibular specialists, independent professional development, and attendance at specialist courses in the UK and
overseas.
I was responsible for the supervision of junior colleagues, appraisals, and performance management. I have
supported team members to analyse data, present audits, develop service improvement ideas and report the
findings to managers. I have helped others design posters for conferences and pass these skills onto more junior
team members. In my last position, I supported the entire therapy outpatient department in a major transformation
project to adopt an online documentation system and coordinated two department relocations and changes in
service delivery.
My interest in vestibular rehabilitation as a speciality, led me to become one of the founding members of

tibular physiotherapists. My
strong relationships within the multidisciplinary clinical and research community have been instrumental in
advancing patient care and contributing to impactful research.
Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.

evidence.text:
Title
Finding out what causes dizziness that is triggered when changing position, to speed up access to the right
treatment
Background to the research
Dizziness affects 1-in-3 people at some stage in their lives. The main type is dizziness when changing position,
such as getting into bed and rolling over. It can have a huge impact on a person’s ability to do everyday activities,
limiting how much they move or walk and can cause falls.
To get help, people visit doctors and physiotherapists in a range of healthcare settings including general
practitioner clinics, emergency departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
The main cause of dizziness when changing position, is due to small crystals getting lodged in a part of the inner
ear where they are not meant to be. When the person moves they experience dizziness due to the crystal
moving. Doctors and physiotherapists can relocate these crystals, by moving the person through a series of
specific positions. Sometimes, dizziness when changing position can be caused by brain problems but many
doctors and physiotherapists are less aware of this.
There are several problems doctors and physiotherapists face wh
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
en treating this type of dizziness, specifically:
 Many are unaware that conditions affecting the brain can also cause this type of dizziness and these
conditions need to be treated differently
 We do not know how many treatments are needed to cure an inner ear problem and how often it returns
 We do not know why some people do not get better
Aims of the research
Aim 1 - develop a list of the clinical features that suggest a brain cause of the dizziness
Aim 2 - identify how many treatments are needed and how often it comes back for an inner ear cause of the
dizziness
Aim 3 - explore if there are differences in the inner ear anatomy for those that do not get better with the specific
treatment positions
Design and methods used
Study 1 – I will complete a thorough review of all the research on brain causes of dizziness with position changes.
This information will be combined with the findings from study 2 to create a list of features to identify brain causes
of dizziness.
Study 2 – I will invite 250 people who have dizziness when they change position to take part. They will fill in
questionnaires, have a thorough dizziness assessment, and treatment when appropriate. Information on
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

the
number of treatments required, and recurrence will be collected.
Study 3 – I will invite 25 people that get better with treatment, and 25 people that do not get better, to have scans
of their inner ears. 3D models will be made from the scans and compared for any differences in the anatomy of
the inner ears.
Patient and public involvement
Ten people with experience of this type of dizziness, expressed frustrations in getting a diagnosis quickly, and
access to the right treatment. Their feedback has shaped the research question and study design, specifically:
 How to advertise the study
 Printing materials, instead of relying on email alone
 Providing interpreters
 Refunding travel costs
Another group of eight patients have agreed to help with recruitment and share the results widely.
Dissemination
Doctors and physiotherapists are the key audience to share these results with, to raise awareness of the causes
and treatment of dizziness caused by changing position, to improve diagnosis and treatment. This will be done
through publications in healthcare journals, presentations at international conferences and courses.
The results will also be shared with patients and public usin

evidence.text:
Background
Positional dizziness accounts for nearly 50% of dizziness cases seeking medical attention. It describes dizziness,
vertigo and unsteadiness triggered by changes in head position, and heavily impacts patients’ independence,
mood, and falls risk.
Patients present across a range of healthcare settings, including general practitioner’s clinics, emergency
departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
Most cases are due to five subtypes of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), but brain (central) disorders
such as tumours, stroke, or vestibular migraine are under-recognised causes of positional dizziness. It is
important to determine the cause to ensure expedient management because the treatments provided by doctors
and physiotherapists for BPPV will not work for central causes and they may require emergency treatment.
Patients in a focus group expressed frustrations at getting a diagnosis and access to prompt and effective
treatment, reinforcing the major challenges faced by doctors and physiotherapists in this area, specifically:
 poor awareness of the diagnostic features of central causes of positional dizziness
 limited evidence on th

e number of treatments required to resolve each BPPV subtype and the recurrence
rates for each BPPV subtype
 uncertainty regarding reasons for poor treatment response and whether an anatomical variation of the
inner ear could explain this
This research intends to address these gaps in knowledge to refine diagnostic frameworks, improve access to
timely effective treatment by doctors and physiotherapists, and improve patient quality of life.
Aims and objectives
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

e correct but these tests rely on correct
execution and interpretation of the induced eye movement (nystagmus). This can be challenging for doctors or
physiotherapists that have little or no training in managing dizziness9 where these patients may present.
It is important to identify whether positional dizziness is due to a peripheral or central disorder because treatments
differ. Repositioning manoeuvres treat and resolve most peripheral causes8. Whereas in central disorders these
do not work, and medication is typically used to reduce symptoms, alongside investigation to address underlying
sinister causes that may require urgent intervention (e.g. brain tumours)2,10.
Furthermore, when BPPV is identified, there is a lack of evidence to guide how many treatments are needed, what
the expected recurrence rates are, and why some patients do not get better.
This research fellowship aims to build on the existing literature and address these critical gaps in understanding,
which could refine diagnostic frameworks, and improve treatment outcomes. This aligns with patient priorities for
quicker, more accurate diagnoses and the broader need for evidence-based management of positional dizzin

e number of treatments required to resolve each BPPV subtype and the recurrence
rates for each BPPV subtype
 uncertainty regarding reasons for poor treatment response and whether an anatomical variation of the
inner ear could explain this
This research intends to address these gaps in knowledge to refine diagnostic frameworks, improve access to
timely effective treatment by doctors and physiotherapists, and improve patient quality of life.
Aims and objectives
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment
chair, wearing video-goggles to record and quantify eye movements. Data will be collected on BPPV treatments
needed and recurrence rates.
Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV
treatment in study 2. Reconstruct three-dimensional models from magnetic resonance imaging scans of the inner
ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment
chair, wearing video-goggles to record and quantify eye movements. Data will be collected on BPPV treatments
needed and recurrence rates.
Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV
treatment in study 2. Reconstruct three-dimensional models from magnetic resonance imaging scans of the inner
ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

uctural causes were omitted yet it is recognised that 90%
of people in a vestibular migraine attack could have positional dizziness10. Therefore, using PICO framework, I will
undertake a comprehensive and up-to-date systematic review that includes adults with positional dizziness (P),
confirmed by positional manoeuvres (I), compared to peripheral positional dizziness (C), to identify the diagnostic
features and associated symptoms for central positional dizziness (O).
I will register the systematic review with PROSPERO and meet with a UCL librarian to ensure a comprehensive
literature search is conducted of key databases.
The articles will be screened against predefined inclusion criteria in two stages, in collaboration with my research
supervisor (Professor Kaski). Initially, the titles and abstracts will be screened and then any excluded will have the
full text reviewed. Disagreements will be settled by a third reviewer, who is a member of the supervisory team with
expertise in undertaking systematic reviews (Dr Ellmers).
Included articles will be assessed for quality by two reviewers to reduce any bias, with a third reviewer involved if
there are any disagreements. Two reviewers

Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.

 poor awareness of the diagnostic features of central causes of positional dizziness
 limited evidence on th

izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment

Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV

This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled

eir efficacy3,8. These manoeuvres aim to
reposition displaced debris from the affected semicircular canal by moving the patient’s head and body through
specific positions. Each position leverages gravity to clear debris from the canal, assuming a standard canal
orientation across all individuals.
Mathematical modelling indicates there are anatomical variations in canal geometry, which may explain why
approximately 10% of patients do not respond to treatment17. However, no imaging studies have investigated if
there is a variation that could account for treatment non-response.
This unexplored area represents a critical gap in our understanding of BPPV management and those that do not
respond. Thus, in work package 3, a subgroup of 50 participants (see sample size section) will have exploratory
3-dimensional models created from MRI scans of the inner ear. Identifying an anatomical variation could inform
future studies and refine the treatment manoeuvre positions to improve treatment outcome, reduce falls risk and
mortality4,5. These were research priorities agreed by the patient focus group, to improve the speed and accuracy
of diagnosis and treatment.
4. What are the research questio

by recent data from the UCLH acute vertigo service, where 240 patients with
dizziness (48 with positional dizziness) were seen in six months. Additional sources, including GP surgeries,
community teams, and self-referrals, make this target achievable over 18 months.
Participant recruitment
The study will be advertised through the NCL Primary Care network and community services for North Central
London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology teams in Barnet, Camden,
Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised in NCL emergency departments, NCL falls services and
internally at UCLH by email and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad recruitment strategy will ensure
the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness. These questions are adopted from a
diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of dizziness23, which was developed with patient involvement. Patients
guided the recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a telephone option on the
advertisements, both of which will be included.

specialist Neuro-otologist. It will expand on the systematic review to establish diagnostic differences between
peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness. It will also address critical gaps in the published literature on
the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV. The overall aim is to improve

or central causes, priorities reported by the patient focus group.
Population
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, which includes white, Asian, and
Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London are Turkish, Irish, Polish, and Asian.
NCL is the second most deprived integrated care system in London19. These factors have been accounted for in
the development of this study, so that funding is available for participants travel and the provision of interpreters.
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The N

paper-based consent will be obtained and stored in a central REDCap database set up for this
project. Participants will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment at the earliest point in time and their
GP informed of their participation.
Participant support
Potential participants can request the information sheet and consent form in paper format, as suggested by two
members of the patient focus group who did not have access to a computer. The signed paper copy of consent
will then be loaded into the secure database by the research team.
Potential participants who do not speak adequate English, will be able to either ask a family member to translate,
or request an interpreter is provided for them throughout this study. Access to an interpreter was included on
request of the patient focus group. Visual aids and a clear explanation of the manoeuvres and tests will be given
to each interpreter to translate.
To maximise participation of under-served groups24, participants will be able to select the time they visit the
hospital and travel costs will be remunerated promptly after each visit. Any non-attender will be contacted to
ensure any barriers to participation are mitiga

ted, with another appointment arranged if they wish to continue.
However, participants have the right to withdraw from the study at anytime without adversely affecting their
ongoing NHS care.
Data Management
Participants’ data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and scanning, but will be anonymised for any
dissemination. All data will be kept confidential and securely stored electronically, requiring a password to access
which will only be known by the research team. Data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine data
governance regulations.
Assessment visit
Participants will complete routine validated questionnaires used in dizziness and balance services (i.e. DHI, SVQ,
STAI-form Y, and VIMSSQ-short) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will help
identify any accompanying or distinguishing features of peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness.
Protected characteristics will also be collected to check for equality and inclusivity of those being recruited. These
will be reviewed monthly and discussed with the patient advisory group and The NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead, to help address any gaps in particip

evidence.text:
Title
Finding out what causes dizziness that is triggered when changing position, to speed up access to the right
treatment
Background to the research
Dizziness affects 1-in-3 people at some stage in their lives. The main type is dizziness when changing position,
such as getting into bed and rolling over. It can have a huge impact on a person’s ability to do everyday activities,
limiting how much they move or walk and can cause falls.
To get help, people visit doctors and physiotherapists in a range of healthcare settings including general
practitioner clinics, emergency departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
The main cause of dizziness when changing position, is due to small crystals getting lodged in a part of the inner
ear where they are not meant to be. When the person moves they experience dizziness due to the crystal
moving. Doctors and physiotherapists can relocate these crystals, by moving the person through a series of
specific positions. Sometimes, dizziness when changing position can be caused by brain problems but many
doctors and physiotherapists are less aware of this.
There are several problems doctors and physiotherapists face wh
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
en treating this type of dizziness, specifically:
 Many are unaware that conditions affecting the brain can also cause this type of dizziness and these
conditions need to be treated differently
 We do not know how many treatments are needed to cure an inner ear problem and how often it returns
 We do not know why some people do not get better
Aims of the research
Aim 1 - develop a list of the clinical features that suggest a brain cause of the dizziness
Aim 2 - identify how many treatments are needed and how often it comes back for an inner ear cause of the
dizziness
Aim 3 - explore if there are differences in the inner ear anatomy for those that do not get better with the specific
treatment positions
Design and methods used
Study 1 – I will complete a thorough review of all the research on brain causes of dizziness with position changes.
This information will be combined with the findings from study 2 to create a list of features to identify brain causes
of dizziness.
Study 2 – I will invite 250 people who have dizziness when they change position to take part. They will fill in
questionnaires, have a thorough dizziness assessment, and treatment when appropriate. Information on
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

the
number of treatments required, and recurrence will be collected.
Study 3 – I will invite 25 people that get better with treatment, and 25 people that do not get better, to have scans
of their inner ears. 3D models will be made from the scans and compared for any differences in the anatomy of
the inner ears.
Patient and public involvement

evidence.text:
Title
Finding out what causes dizziness that is triggered when changing position, to speed up access to the right
treatment
Background to the research
Dizziness affects 1-in-3 people at some stage in their lives. The main type is dizziness when changing position,
such as getting into bed and rolling over. It can have a huge impact on a person’s ability to do everyday activities,
limiting how much they move or walk and can cause falls.
To get help, people visit doctors and physiotherapists in a range of healthcare settings including general
practitioner clinics, emergency departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
The main cause of dizziness when changing position, is due to small crystals getting lodged in a part of the inner
ear where they are not meant to be. When the person moves they experience dizziness due to the crystal
moving. Doctors and physiotherapists can relocate these crystals, by moving the person through a series of
specific positions. Sometimes, dizziness when changing position can be caused by brain problems but many
doctors and physiotherapists are less aware of this.
There are several problems doctors and physiotherapists face wh

Aim 2 - identify how many treatments are needed and how often it comes back for an inner ear cause of the

evidence.text:
Background
Positional dizziness accounts for nearly 50% of dizziness cases seeking medical attention. It describes dizziness,
vertigo and unsteadiness triggered by changes in head position, and heavily impacts patients’ independence,
mood, and falls risk.
Patients present across a range of healthcare settings, including general practitioner’s clinics, emergency
departments, physiotherapy services, falls services and specialist clinics.
Most cases are due to five subtypes of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV), but brain (central) disorders
such as tumours, stroke, or vestibular migraine are under-recognised causes of positional dizziness. It is
important to determine the cause to ensure expedient management because the treatments provided by doctors
and physiotherapists for BPPV will not work for central causes and they may require emergency treatment.
Patients in a focus group expressed frustrations at getting a diagnosis and access to prompt and effective
treatment, reinforcing the major challenges faced by doctors and physiotherapists in this area, specifically:
 poor awareness of the diagnostic features of central causes of positional dizziness
 limited evidence on th

e number of treatments required to resolve each BPPV subtype and the recurrence
rates for each BPPV subtype
 uncertainty regarding reasons for poor treatment response and whether an anatomical variation of the
inner ear could explain this
This research intends to address these gaps in knowledge to refine diagnostic frameworks, improve access to
timely effective treatment by doctors and physiotherapists, and improve patient quality of life.
Aims and objectives
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

e correct but these tests rely on correct
execution and interpretation of the induced eye movement (nystagmus). This can be challenging for doctors or
physiotherapists that have little or no training in managing dizziness9 where these patients may present.
It is important to identify whether positional dizziness is due to a peripheral or central disorder because treatments
differ. Repositioning manoeuvres treat and resolve most peripheral causes8. Whereas in central disorders these
do not work, and medication is typically used to reduce symptoms, alongside investigation to address underlying
sinister causes that may require urgent intervention (e.g. brain tumours)2,10.
Furthermore, when BPPV is identified, there is a lack of evidence to guide how many treatments are needed, what
the expected recurrence rates are, and why some patients do not get better.
This research fellowship aims to build on the existing literature and address these critical gaps in understanding,
which could refine diagnostic frameworks, and improve treatment outcomes. This aligns with patient priorities for
quicker, more accurate diagnoses and the broader need for evidence-based management of positional dizzin

criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-defined and realistic.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks identified; the scope is well-defined and realistic.
