This research intends to address these gaps in knowledge to refine diagnostic frameworks, improve access to
timely effective treatment by doctors and physiotherapists, and improve patient quality of life.
Aims and objectives
 Review (i) the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness and (ii) explore
which differentiate central from peripheral dizziness
 Characterise the diagnostic features for peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness
 Determine how many treatments are required and the recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV
 Explore possible anatomical differences to the inner ear affecting treatment response
Methods
Study 1 – Complete a systematic review to identify the reported diagnostic characteristics for central causes of
positional dizziness, to incorporate into study 2.
Study 2 – Recruit 250 adults living in North Central London, reporting positional dizziness. Participants will
complete d

izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment
chair, wearing video-goggles to record and quantify eye movements. Data will be collected on BPPV treatments
needed and recurrence rates.
Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV
treatment in study 2. Reconstruct three-dimensional models from magnetic resonance imaging scans of the inner
ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

5. Project Plan
This fellowship will be formed of three interconnected work packages.
Work package 1: Systematic review
Objectives:
 Systematically review the nystagmus characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness
 Systematically review the patient risk factors and associated symptoms for central causes of positional
dizziness
 Explore what characteristics, risk factors and associated symptoms distinguish central from peripheral
causes of positional dizziness

included literature was restricted to three years. Non-str

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods

criteria.rationale: While sample sizes are defined, detailed power calculations or specific statistical controls for the secondary analyses are not explicitly detailed in the provided text chunks, though the training plan addresses statistical skills.

criteria.pros: The application articulates clear benefits including reduced falls risk, improved diagnostic accuracy, and reduced NHS strain (secdrp__001_d, secsa__001_a). It highlights potential to enhance patient quality of life and aligns with health priorities regarding dizziness management (secarb__001_f).

evidence.text:
tibular physiotherapists. My
strong relationships within the multidisciplinary clinical and research community have been instrumental in
advancing patient care and contributing to impactful research.
Securing this fellowship will empower me to pursue research on a topic I am deeply passionate about, whilst
addressing the most common cause of dizziness worldwide. This work has the potential to significantly enhance
patients' quality of life while alleviating the strain on overburdened NHS services. The fellowship will provide
essential resources, dedicated time, and access to leading research experts, ensuring the project is not only
feasible but also methodologically rigorous.
Towards the end of this fellowship, I hope to establish a part time NHS position at UCLH. This will allow the new
referral pathway and service to continue upon study completion, whilst I apply for an NIHR advanced clinical and
practitioner academic fellowship. This fellowship therefore represents a crucial step in realising my vision to drive
meaningful change in vestibular health on both national and global scales and advance my academic career.
Have you already
No
registered for a PhD?
Has this application,

or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
This research project was submitted to the Meniere’s Society, the UK’s balance and dizziness charity, to request
research funding. The application was peer reviewed by several external experts and layperson committee
members, and feedback was given to the trustees. A decision was given in December 2024 to award £55,106.04
towards the project. The award letter stated they felt the project was “…important and would be very beneficial for
patients with dizziness and unsteadiness”.
Unfortunately, as they are a small charity with limited funds and receive numerous grant applications each year,
they were unable to award the full amount requested. However, they are very enthusiastic about a joint
collaboration with the NIHR and fully support this research fellowship application. They are keen to help
throughout the project including with recruitment and dissemination via presentations, their newsletter and at their
balance awareness week events.
This fellowship application reflects the total cost of £447,139.00 that is required to undertake this research project,

Positional dizziness is a prevalent complaint in healthcare1,3, yet it is frequently misdiagnosed6, and can have life-
threatening consequences1,7 . Frequent delays in treatment3,11 results in increased strain on healthcare
resources3,11 life3.
and greater impact on patient quality of By developing a practical and evidence-based
diagnostic and treatment framework, this programme of work has the potential to:
Enhance Diagnostic Accuracy: Equip clinicians with tools to differentiate peripheral from central causes of
positional dizziness10, ensuring patients receive timely and appropriate treatment.
Support Healthcare Providers: Provide clinicians with accessible, evidence-based tools to confidently diagnose
and manage positional dizziness, reducing misdiagnosis6 and unnecessary referrals11.
Improve Quality of Life: Accurate and early diagnosis and treatment will enable patients to return to work, live
independently, and fully engage with family and social activities3. This will significantly reduce the mental an

ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

eir efficacy3,8. These manoeuvres aim to
reposition displaced debris from the affected semicircular canal by moving the patient’s head and body through
specific positions. Each position leverages gravity to clear debris from the canal, assuming a standard canal
orientation across all individuals.
Mathematical modelling indicates there are anatomical variations in canal geometry, which may explain why
approximately 10% of patients do not respond to treatment17. However, no imaging studies have investigated if
there is a variation that could account for treatment non-response.
This unexplored area represents a critical gap in our understanding of BPPV management and those that do not
respond. Thus, in work package 3, a subgroup of 50 participants (see sample size section) will have exploratory
3-dimensional models created from MRI scans of the inner ear. Identifying an anatomical variation could inform
future studies and refine the treatment manoeuvre positions to improve treatment outcome, reduce falls risk and
mortality4,5. These were research priorities agreed by the patient focus group, to improve the speed and accuracy
of diagnosis and treatment.
4. What are the research questio

by recent data from the UCLH acute vertigo service, where 240 patients with
dizziness (48 with positional dizziness) were seen in six months. Additional sources, including GP surgeries,
community teams, and self-referrals, make this target achievable over 18 months.
Participant recruitment
The study will be advertised through the NCL Primary Care network and community services for North Central
London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology teams in Barnet, Camden,
Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised in NCL emergency departments, NCL falls services and
internally at UCLH by email and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad recruitment strategy will ensure
the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness. These questions are adopted from a
diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of dizziness23, which was developed with patient involvement. Patients
guided the recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a telephone option on the
advertisements, both of which will be included.

or a similar application, previously been submitted to this or any other funding body?
Yes
If Yes, provide details
This research project was submitted to the Meniere’s Society, the UK’s balance and dizziness charity, to request
research funding. The application was peer reviewed by several external experts and layperson committee
members, and feedback was given to the trustees. A decision was given in December 2024 to award £55,106.04
towards the project. The award letter stated they felt the project was “…important and would be very beneficial for
patients with dizziness and unsteadiness”.
Unfortunately, as they are a small charity with limited funds and receive numerous grant applications each year,
they were unable to award the full amount requested. However, they are very enthusiastic about a joint
collaboration with the NIHR and fully support this research fellowship application. They are keen to help
throughout the project including with recruitment and dissemination via presentations, their newsletter and at their
balance awareness week events.
This fellowship application reflects the total cost of £447,139.00 that is required to undertake this research project,
remunerate

patient and public involvement, undertake relevant professional and academic training, attend
conferences, and complete a three year full-time PhD. The amount awarded by the Meniere’s Society of
£55,106.04, will be deducted from the total cost, should this application be successful.
This application has also been reviewed by the NIHR research support service hub and underwent a
grantsmanship review. All feedback has been taken onboard and addressed and I believe this has strengthened
the application.

Study 3 – I will invite 25 people that get better with treatment, and 25 people that do not get better, to have scans
of their inner ears. 3D models will be made from the scans and compared for any differences in the anatomy of
the inner ears.
Patient and public involvement
Ten people with experience of this type of dizziness, expressed frustrations in getting a diagnosis quickly, and
access to the right treatment. Their feedback has shaped the research question and study design, specifically:
 How to advertise the study
 Printing materials, instead of relying on email alone
 Providing interpreters
 Refunding travel costs
Another group of eight patients have agreed to help with recruitment and share the results widely.
Dissemination
Doctors and physiotherapists are the key audience to share these results with, to raise awareness of the causes
and treatment of dizziness caused by changing position, to improve diagnosis and treatment. This will be done
through publications in healthcare journals, presentations at international conferences and courses.
The results will also be shared with patients and public usin

evidence.text:
izziness related questionnaires and undergo a standardised assessment using a specialist treatment
chair, wearing video-goggles to record and quantify eye movements. Data will be collected on BPPV treatments
needed and recurrence rates.
Study 3 – Conduct a pilot study to explore if an anatomical variation accounts for non-response to BPPV
treatment in study 2. Reconstruct three-dimensional models from magnetic resonance imaging scans of the inner
ear, and compare 25 participants that respond to treatment, to 25 that do not.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will impact doctors and physiotherapists managing patients with positional dizziness, but also patient
wellbeing. Diagnostic features for central positional dizziness will be identified and existing knowledge gaps filled
for BPPV, specifically the number of treatments needed, recurrence rates and why some patients do not get
better. Findings will be published in medical journals and presented at conferences and courses, to inform
diagnostic and treatment guidelines, and improve patient care. An infographic and dedicated workshop will help
share the results with patients.

and those that do not fulfil the
diagnostic criteria for BPPV or present with abnormalities in the eye examination referred for further review by a
specialist Neuro-otologist. It will expand on the systematic review to establish diagnostic differences between
peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness. It will also address critical gaps in the published literature on
the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV. The overall aim is to improve
accurate assessment and early treatment for patients with positional dizziness, whether that be due to peripheral
or central causes, priorities reported by the patient focus group.
Population
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, which includes white, Asian, and
Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London are Turkish, Irish, Polish, and Asian.
NCL is the second most deprived integrated care system in London19. These factors have been accounted for in
the development of this study, so that funding is available for participants travel and the provision of interpreters.
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The N

paper-based consent will be obtained and stored in a central REDCap database set up for this
project. Participants will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment at the earliest point in time and their
GP informed of their participation.
Participant support
Potential participants can request the information sheet and consent form in paper format, as suggested by two
members of the patient focus group who did not have access to a computer. The signed paper copy of consent
will then be loaded into the secure database by the research team.
Potential participants who do not speak adequate English, will be able to either ask a family member to translate,
or request an interpreter is provided for them throughout this study. Access to an interpreter was included on
request of the patient focus group. Visual aids and a clear explanation of the manoeuvres and tests will be given
to each interpreter to translate.
To maximise participation of under-served groups24, participants will be able to select the time they visit the
hospital and travel costs will be remunerated promptly after each visit. Any non-attender will be contacted to
ensure any barriers to participation are mitiga

ted, with another appointment arranged if they wish to continue.
However, participants have the right to withdraw from the study at anytime without adversely affecting their
ongoing NHS care.
Data Management
Participants’ data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and scanning, but will be anonymised for any
dissemination. All data will be kept confidential and securely stored electronically, requiring a password to access
which will only be known by the research team. Data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine data
governance regulations.
Assessment visit
Participants will complete routine validated questionnaires used in dizziness and balance services (i.e. DHI, SVQ,
STAI-form Y, and VIMSSQ-short) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will help
identify any accompanying or distinguishing features of peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness.
Protected characteristics will also be collected to check for equality and inclusivity of those being recruited. These
will be reviewed monthly and discussed with the patient advisory group and The NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead, to help address any gaps in particip

Proposed training and development programme
Proposed formal study
This PhD will be registered at UCL, and I will attend free workshops provided as part of their doctoral skills training
program. These include research governance and copyright, public engagement and impact, data visualisation,
thesis writing and advancing my presentation skills. These sessions will be distributed across the three-year
period, as outlined on the GANTT chart.
Academic training plan
To enhance these workshops, I have identified external courses that will help me to gain the skills required to
conduct the proposed research study (see also GANTT chart).
1. Gain skills in conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis
In order to complete work package 1, I will attend a four-day course “Introduction to systematic review and meta-
analysis” provided by the University of Bristol (£880.00). This course will teach the methodology of a systematic
review, how to perform a comprehensive literature review, how to evaluate the quality of a systematic review, and
how to summarise the findings of a systematic review and meta-analysis. I will also seek support from my
supervisory team, which includes a researcher w
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ith experience in systematic reviews and meta-analyses (Dr
Ellmers).
2. Develop skills in Epidemiology
In work package 2, I will collect a wide range of data, from questionnaires and clinical assessment in order to build
predictive models. I will therefore attend a 15-day in-person course “Introductory Course in Epidemiology and
Medical Statistics”, provided by London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (£3700.00). This course will
teach me the concepts of epidemiology, statistical analysis and regression, data analysis and interpretation.
3. Develop skills in data analysis
I envisage collecting a large range of clinical data in work package 2, which I have not handled in previous
research. Therefore, I will attend the four-day course “Practical statistics for medical research” provided by UCL
(£750.00). This will provide the knowledge and skills to select, execute and interpret the required data analysis.
In addition, I will attend the two-day course “Data analytics with Python: Introduction” provided by City Lit
(£249.00). This will help me to learn how to use coding to organise, examine and analyse data, which will be a
useful skill when handling large datasets.
4. Gain skil
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ls in assessing and treating positional dizziness
I will increase my experience in using a specialist chair (Rotundum chair) that standardises the diagnosis and
treatment of positional dizziness, alongside developing skills in interpreting the nystagmus collected by the video
goggles. These will be essential skills to develop for work package 2, as this will be the main data collection
method.
I have approached Dr Dan Dupont Hougaard, at Aalborg University Hospital in Denmark. He is an internationally
renowned ENT specialist with expertise in using mechanical repositioning chairs for over 10 years and is the most
published author on the topic. He has invited me to spend time within his clinic to gain skills in using the
Rotundum chair and in collecting and interpreting nystagmus recorded by video-nystagmography.
Following this overseas learning opportunity, I will join monthly clinics led by audiology and neurology colleagues
in the acute vertigo team at UCLH. This will provide additional opportunities to observe experts assessing patients
with positional dizziness and provide practice in interpreting the nystagmus captured by video-nystagmography. I
will collaborate with them to v
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
erify my analysis of the nystagmus recordings, enhancing my accuracy and ensuring
the data collection methods are robust.
5. 3D modelling
In order to complete work package 3, I need to learn skills in using specialist software to reconstruct 3D models to
make comparisons. Therefore, I will attend training provided by an academic radiologist (Dr Bray) and complete
online training to gain these skills. I will also collaborate with Dr Bray to verify my initial analyses are accurate.
6. Acquire skills to maximise knowledge translation
To make a meaningful difference to the management of positional dizziness, I need to ensure the findings from
this fellowship, and future studies I design as the next part of my NIHR academic pathway, are successfully
implemented. Therefore, towards the end of this fellowship, I will attend a six-week, part time course on
“Introduction to Implementation science” provided by Kings College London (£1400.00). This course will provide
skills to help with dissemination, engage stakeholders and design future studies.
Additionally, I wish to include patients and public in disseminating the research findings, to ensure they are
communicated effectively, in unders

rt of the balance awareness week in

costs have been calculated using the NIHR payment guidance of £50 per hour due to them receiving tasks that
require small amounts of preparation.

7. Develop confident skills in research leadership
The fellowship will be crucial to allow me to complete a PhD and progress my clinical academic career. I am keen
to build on my experience in clinical leadership, with a view to lead a team of researchers to continue investigating
positional dizziness and expand on the findings from this proposal. I will therefore attend the free “Resilient
Leadership in Action” course provided by UCL. This course is designed for early-stage career researchers and will
help me understand my leadership style, help to set up an effective research team and equip me with the skills to
lead that research team to maximise research impact.

treatment of the most common cause of dizziness and enhance my ability to triage an

I will also maintain my strong connections within special interest groups within physiotherapy (ACPIVR and

RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Diego Kaski
SENSE Research Unit, Department of Clinical and Movement
Institution
Neurosciences, Institute of Neurology
Position Consultant Neurologist and Honorary Associate Professor
Description of support provided
Professor Diego Kaski is the Director of the SENSE Research Unit within the Department of Clinical and
Movement Neurosciences at the Institute of Neurology. His work focuses on the translational research to develop
novel therapies for patients with neurological balance disorders. His research programme aims to improve clinical
outcomes in
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
patients with vestibular and neurological balance disorders, focusing on novel clinical biomarkers to
monitor disease progression and treatment response, and design and implementation of treatment strategies.
He is one of the only Neurologists in the UK with expertise in central positional dizziness and is therefore
strategically placed to oversee this fellowship project. He will be providing research support focusing on study
design, recruitment strategy, research ethics, and ensuring successful completion of the study. He will also be
supporting the development of Amanda’s research, teaching and clinical skills, particularly with respect to career
satisfaction, career management and progression.
His background as a clinical-academic neurologist, successful research trajectory alongside his clinical expertise,
and established track record in working with numerous undergraduate and postgraduate students to completion
makes him an ideal supervisor for Amanda. He has overseen successful completion of 2 PhD students, and
currently has 4 PhD students as primary and 2 as secondary supervisor.
Professor Kaski holds monthly lab meetings, which Amanda will attend. He will also be holding 1

meetings with the applicant throughout the Fellowship and will be available most days to discuss any concerns or

Name Dr Gita Ramdharry
Institution National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery
Position Consultant Allied Health Professional in Neuromuscular Diseases
Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a Consultant Allied Health Professional specialising in neuromuscular diseases at the
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, UCLH, having begun her career as a clinical physiotherapist.
She has been affiliated with UCLH for over 20 years. She is the principal investigator of the Neuromuscular
Rehabilitation Research Group and has a special interest in balance in these cohorts.
Dr Ramdharry possesses significant research and statistics experience and holds academic appointments as an
Honorary Associate Professor at UCL. She has a strong understanding of the NIHR landscape, particularly the
Integrated Clinical and Practitioner Academic (ICA) programmes and is currently serving as a Deputy Chair of the
ICA Clinical Doctoral Awards panel. Her considerable supervisory experience includes m
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

will help her conceptualise and deliver a top-quality NIHR post-doctoral fellowship
application to ensure she continues on a clinical academic path in the longer term.
As the host organisation, we are committed to ensuring Ms Male’s success as a clinical academic researcher
throughout her fellowship and beyond. We believe that this fellowship will significantly contribute to the
advancement of healthcare and the development of a highly qualified clinical academic researcher.

criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

criteria.pros: The application provides a highly detailed training plan covering seven distinct areas (systematic review, epidemiology, data analysis, clinical skills, 3D modelling, implementation science, leadership). Each training activity is explicitly linked to specific work packages (e.g., systematic review for WP1, epidemiology for WP2) and includes specific course names, providers, and costs. The plan is well-structured with clear descriptions of the skills to be acquired.
criteria.drawbacks: While the text mentions a GANTT chart for distribution across the three-year period, the specific timeline sequencing of the training activities within the text itself is descriptive rather than a visual or tabular schedule, preventing a perfect score for structured timeline evidence.
criteria.rationale: While the text mentions a GANTT chart for distribution across the three-year period, the specific timeline sequencing of the training activities within the text itself is descriptive rather than a visual or tabular schedule, preventing a perfect score for structured timeline evidence.

evidence.text:
Proposed training and development programme
Proposed formal study
This PhD will be registered at UCL, and I will attend free workshops provided as part of their doctoral skills training
program. These include research governance and copyright, public engagement and impact, data visualisation,
thesis writing and advancing my presentation skills. These sessions will be distributed across the three-year
period, as outlined on the GANTT chart.
Academic training plan
To enhance these workshops, I have identified external courses that will help me to gain the skills required to
conduct the proposed research study (see also GANTT chart).
1. Gain skills in conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis
In order to complete work package 1, I will attend a four-day course “Introduction to systematic review and meta-
analysis” provided by the University of Bristol (£880.00). This course will teach the methodology of a systematic
review, how to perform a comprehensive literature review, how to evaluate the quality of a systematic review, and
how to summarise the findings of a systematic review and meta-analysis. I will also seek support from my
supervisory team, which includes a researcher w
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ith experience in systematic reviews and meta-analyses (Dr
Ellmers).
2. Develop skills in Epidemiology
In work package 2, I will collect a wide range of data, from questionnaires and clinical assessment in order to build
predictive models. I will therefore attend a 15-day in-person course “Introductory Course in Epidemiology and
Medical Statistics”, provided by London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (£3700.00). This course will
teach me the concepts of epidemiology, statistical analysis and regression, data analysis and interpretation.
3. Develop skills in data analysis
I envisage collecting a large range of clinical data in work package 2, which I have not handled in previous
research. Therefore, I will attend the four-day course “Practical statistics for medical research” provided by UCL
(£750.00). This will provide the knowledge and skills to select, execute and interpret the required data analysis.
In addition, I will attend the two-day course “Data analytics with Python: Introduction” provided by City Lit
(£249.00). This will help me to learn how to use coding to organise, examine and analyse data, which will be a
useful skill when handling large datasets.
4. Gain skil
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ls in assessing and treating positional dizziness
I will increase my experience in using a specialist chair (Rotundum chair) that standardises the diagnosis and
treatment of positional dizziness, alongside developing skills in interpreting the nystagmus collected by the video
goggles. These will be essential skills to develop for work package 2, as this will be the main data collection
method.
I have approached Dr Dan Dupont Hougaard, at Aalborg University Hospital in Denmark. He is an internationally
renowned ENT specialist with expertise in using mechanical repositioning chairs for over 10 years and is the most
published author on the topic. He has invited me to spend time within his clinic to gain skills in using the
Rotundum chair and in collecting and interpreting nystagmus recorded by video-nystagmography.
Following this overseas learning opportunity, I will join monthly clinics led by audiology and neurology colleagues
in the acute vertigo team at UCLH. This will provide additional opportunities to observe experts assessing patients
with positional dizziness and provide practice in interpreting the nystagmus captured by video-nystagmography. I
will collaborate with them to v
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
erify my analysis of the nystagmus recordings, enhancing my accuracy and ensuring
the data collection methods are robust.
5. 3D modelling
In order to complete work package 3, I need to learn skills in using specialist software to reconstruct 3D models to
make comparisons. Therefore, I will attend training provided by an academic radiologist (Dr Bray) and complete
online training to gain these skills. I will also collaborate with Dr Bray to verify my initial analyses are accurate.
6. Acquire skills to maximise knowledge translation
To make a meaningful difference to the management of positional dizziness, I need to ensure the findings from
this fellowship, and future studies I design as the next part of my NIHR academic pathway, are successfully
implemented. Therefore, towards the end of this fellowship, I will attend a six-week, part time course on
“Introduction to Implementation science” provided by Kings College London (£1400.00). This course will provide
skills to help with dissemination, engage stakeholders and design future studies.
Additionally, I wish to include patients and public in disseminating the research findings, to ensure they are
communicated effectively, in unders
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
rt of the balance awareness week in
September 2028. In addition, the eight patient advisory group members will be reimbursed for their time. These
costs have been calculated using the NIHR payment guidance of £50 per hour due to them receiving tasks that
require small amounts of preparation.

Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

tandable language and reach those that are affected. I will therefore attend a
free, four-week, part-time course on “Public Involvement in Research” provided by Imperial College London. This
course will ensure I maximise the skills of the patient advisory group and other collaborators for dissemination.
7. Develop confident skills in research leadership
The fellowship will be crucial to allow me to complete a PhD and progress my clinical academic career. I am keen
to build on my experience in clinical leadership, with a view to lead a team of researchers to continue investigating
positional dizziness and expand on the findings from this proposal. I will therefore attend the free “Resilient
Leadership in Action” course provided by UCL. This course is designed for early-stage career researchers and will
help me understand my leadership style, help to set up an effective research team and equip me with the skills to
lead that research team to maximise research impact.
Career development training
I will undertake this research fellowship full-time. Work package 2 will advance my skills in assessment and
treatment of the most common cause of dizziness and enhance my ability to triage an

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides extensive evidence of the contracting organisation's and supervisors' track records. Specific details include Professor Kaski's role as Director of the SENSE Research Unit, Dr. Ramdharry's 20+ years at UCLH and NIHR panel experience, Dr. Ellmers' Promising Scientist Award and recent guidelines, and Dr. Bray's >£3.5m grant funding and 40+ publications. The text explicitly lists PhD completions and current supervisory loads for all four supervisors.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While individual supervisor achievements are detailed, the specific institutional track record of UCL in this exact niche (beyond the supervisors' individual reputations) is inferred rather than explicitly quantified with institutional-level metrics (e.g., departmental grant totals), though the supervisors' credentials strongly imply institutional excellence.
sub_criteria.rationale: While individual supervisor achievements are detailed, the specific institutional track record of UCL in this exact niche (beyond the supervisors' individual reputations) is inferred rather than explicitly quantified with institutional-level metrics (e.g., departmental grant totals), though the supervisors' credentials strongly imply institutional excellence.

Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a Consultant Allied Health Professional specialising in neuromuscular diseases at the
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, UCLH, having begun her career as a clinical physiotherapist.
She has been affiliated with UCLH for over 20 years. She is the principal investigator of the Neuromuscular
Rehabilitation Research Group and has a special interest in balance in these cohorts.
Dr Ramdharry possesses significant research and statistics experience and holds academic appointments as an
Honorary Associate Professor at UCL. She has a strong understanding of the NIHR landscape, particularly the

evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ences, Imperial College
London, where he studies the neuro-cognitive factors contributing to dizziness and imbalance in older adults. In
2023, he received the Promising Scientist Award from the International Society of Posture & Gait Research for his
work.
Dr Ellmers is the co-lead of the ‘Concerns about Falling’ Working Group within the World Falls Guidelines global
initiative. This involved conducting a modified Delphi with ~100 of world experts within falls prevention and
management. As part of this process, Dr Ellmers led a systematic review and meta-analysis exploring if concerns
about falling predict future falls in older adults (manuscript in revision at Age and Ageing).
At Imperial College London, Dr Ellmers works alongside Professor Adolfo Bronstein and Dr Barry Seemungal,
eminent Consultant Neuro-Otologists and vestibular scientists. He has recently led the generation of clinical
guidelines for the assessment of positional dizziness in older adult fallers (https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afad206)
and a systematic review and meta-analysis that identified dizziness as a crucial risk factor for falls in older adults
(https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afae177).
Dr Ellmers has ext
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ensive expertise in specialist statistical analysis and the research methodologies utilised in this
project (e.g., systematic reviews and meta-analyses, Delphi, large clinical datasets, etc.). He will provide hands-on
research training and support to the applicant on these methodologies throughout the Fellowship. He is currently
the primary supervisor for 3 PhD students and is secondary supervisor for 4 PhD students, 3 of whom are in their
final year.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 4
Name Dr Timothy Bray
Institution UCL Centre for Medical Imaging
Position Academic Consultant Radiologist
Description of support provided
Dr Timothy Bray is an Academic Consultant Radiologist based between the UCL Centre for Medical Imaging
(CMI), the UCL Hawkes Institute and the UCLH Imaging Department. He has an outstanding cross-disciplinary
skillset including both technical developments of novel MRI methods (with a recent patent submission) as well as
MRI translation and radiology. In total, he has over 40 publications including four publications in the top-ranked
MRI methodology journal and multiple top-ranked publications in MRI translation and radiology and has been
awarded >£3.5m grant funding fo
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
r his MRI research.
His unique, interdisciplinary skillset has been recognized through election to the International Society for Magnetic
Resonance in Medicine Junior Fellowship (2019) and Annual meeting Program Committee (2025-2028). He leads
the UCL/UCLH Integrated Academic Training Program in Radiology, and is Clinical Lead for the UCLH MRI
service,. He has supervised two PhD students to completion and currently supervises two more, as well as one
masters and one iBSc student.
He is Deputy Director of the newly-formed UCL Research Imaging Organisation (RIO), which brings together
world-leading infrastructure for imaging research at UCL under a single umbrella, and will help to oversee and
facilitate the developments proposed in this project.
Dr Bray has agreed to supervise this project, and meet regularly throughout the fellowship to support MRI
sequence setup and the development of a processing pipeline; this collaboration will also give me access to world-
leading experts in medical physics and statistics at CMI and RIO, providing an outstanding environment within
which to conduct my research.

strategically placed to oversee this fellowship project. He will be providing research support focusing on study
design, recruitment strategy, research ethics, and ensuring successful completion of the study. He will also be
supporting the development of Amanda’s research, teaching and clinical skills, particularly with respect to career
satisfaction, career management and progression.

Name Dr Gita Ramdharry
Institution National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery
Position Consultant Allied Health Professional in Neuromuscular Diseases
Description of support provided
Dr Gita Ramdharry is a Consultant Allied Health Professional specialising in neuromuscular diseases at the
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, UCLH, having begun her career as a clinical physiotherapist.
She has been affiliated with UCLH for over 20 years. She is the principal investigator of the Neuromuscular
Rehabilitation Research Group and has a special interest in balance in these cohorts.
Dr Ramdharry possesses significant research and statistics experience and holds academic appointments as an
Honorary Associate Professor at UCL. She has a strong understanding of the NIHR landscape, particularly the
Integrated Clinical and Practitioner Academic (ICA) programmes and is currently serving as a Deputy Chair of the
ICA Clinical Doctoral Awards panel. Her considerable supervisory experience includes m
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ences, Imperial College
London, where he studies the neuro-cognitive factors contributing to dizziness and imbalance in older adults. In
2023, he received the Promising Scientist Award from the International Society of Posture & Gait Research for his
work.
Dr Ellmers is the co-lead of the ‘Concerns about Falling’ Working Group within the World Falls Guidelines global
initiative. This involved conducting a modified Delphi with ~100 of world experts within falls prevention and
management. As part of this process, Dr Ellmers led a systematic review and meta-analysis exploring if concerns
about falling predict future falls in older adults (manuscript in revision at Age and Ageing).
At Imperial College London, Dr Ellmers works alongside Professor Adolfo Bronstein and Dr Barry Seemungal,
eminent Consultant Neuro-Otologists and vestibular scientists. He has recently led the generation of clinical
guidelines for the assessment of positional dizziness in older adult fallers (https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afad206)
and a systematic review and meta-analysis that identified dizziness as a crucial risk factor for falls in older adults
(https://doi.org/10.1093/ageing/afae177).

by the range of subspecialty lab members, gender, and ethnicity within the team. We strive to ensure we provide a

evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

rt all new members to abide by the UCL principles of equality, diversity,

training, to enable her to carry out her research project effectively. She will have access to world-leading facilities
and technology in the gait laboratory at 33 Queen Square together, most notably the Rotundum chair that is
integral to her project, with the highest quality training. In addition, we will provide Ms Male with doctoral
mentorship and steer from an internationally recognised clinical academic, Associate Prof Diego Kaski, an expert
in behavioural neurology and vestibular disorders. The Department also benefits from direct links with experienced
researchers and clinicians at Queen Square and Royal Free hospitals to support her research. Ms Male shows
every sign of being at the forefront of her field and we are keen for her to join our department.

If Yes, provide details
This research project was submitted to the Meniere’s Society, the UK’s balance and dizziness charity, to request
research funding. The application was peer reviewed by several external experts and layperson committee
members, and feedback was given to the trustees. A decision was given in December 2024 to award £55,106.04
towards the project. The award letter stated they felt the project was “…important and would be very beneficial for
patients with dizziness and unsteadiness”.
Unfortunately, as they are a small charity with limited funds and receive numerous grant applications each year,
they were unable to award the full amount requested. However, they are very enthusiastic about a joint
collaboration with the NIHR and fully support this research fellowship application. They are keen to help
throughout the project including with recruitment and dissemination via presentations, their newsletter and at their
balance awareness week events.
This fellowship application reflects the total cost of £447,139.00 that is required to undertake this research project,

analysing, findings to share with the patients and public, and professional manuscripts to be written.
I am committed to progressing my career as a clinical academic researcher, and my obligations to disseminate
results from my research activities. As such, I made the decision to leave my full-time NHS position to allow time
to focus on my research and took up a part time, honorary (unpaid) contract role based in the SENSE research
unit, at UCL. This part time role has allowed the time to prepare and successfully publish three articles relating to
the Lily Foundation research grant and assist in one other. It also allowed time to publish one more article relating
to this fellowship topic and be an editor for a standalone supplement in the Journal of laryngology and otology.
Moreover, alongside the research team, I continue to analyse the data to address secondary variables.
In addition, this part time role in the SENSE research unit has provided a rich opportunity to collaborate and
network with current UCL PhD students and other distinguished researchers. It has provided time to undertake
the patient focus group, establish a patient advisory group for this project, and the time t

Patient and public involvement
Ten people with experience of this type of dizziness, expressed frustrations in getting a diagnosis quickly, and
access to the right treatment. Their feedback has shaped the research question and study design, specifically:
 How to advertise the study
 Printing materials, instead of relying on email alone
 Providing interpreters
 Refunding travel costs
Another group of eight patients have agreed to help with recruitment and share the results widely.
Dissemination
Doctors and physiotherapists are the key audience to share these results with, to raise awareness of the causes
and treatment of dizziness caused by changing position, to improve diagnosis and treatment. This will be done
through publications in healthcare journals, presentations at international conferences and courses.

evidence.text:
eir efficacy3,8. These manoeuvres aim to
reposition displaced debris from the affected semicircular canal by moving the patient’s head and body through
specific positions. Each position leverages gravity to clear debris from the canal, assuming a standard canal
orientation across all individuals.
Mathematical modelling indicates there are anatomical variations in canal geometry, which may explain why
approximately 10% of patients do not respond to treatment17. However, no imaging studies have investigated if
there is a variation that could account for treatment non-response.
This unexplored area represents a critical gap in our understanding of BPPV management and those that do not
respond. Thus, in work package 3, a subgroup of 50 participants (see sample size section) will have exploratory
3-dimensional models created from MRI scans of the inner ear. Identifying an anatomical variation could inform
future studies and refine the treatment manoeuvre positions to improve treatment outcome, reduce falls risk and
mortality4,5. These were research priorities agreed by the patient focus group, to improve the speed and accuracy
of diagnosis and treatment.
4. What are the research questio

by recent data from the UCLH acute vertigo service, where 240 patients with
dizziness (48 with positional dizziness) were seen in six months. Additional sources, including GP surgeries,
community teams, and self-referrals, make this target achievable over 18 months.
Participant recruitment
The study will be advertised through the NCL Primary Care network and community services for North Central
London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology teams in Barnet, Camden,
Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised in NCL emergency departments, NCL falls services and
internally at UCLH by email and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad recruitment strategy will ensure
the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness. These questions are adopted from a
diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of dizziness23, which was developed with patient involvement. Patients
guided the recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a telephone option on the
advertisements, both of which will be included.

and anticipated impact
Development of diagnostic criteria
Key diagnostic features for central positional dizziness identified in this project will be presented locally to the NCL
primary care network, and nationally to the British Society of Neuro-Otology, for consultation. The findings will
then be shared internationally to the Barany Society in order to propose diagnostic criteria for central positional
dizziness and refine treatment recommendations for BPPV that doctors and physiotherapists can implement.
Publications
I will submit the results to relevant peer reviewed journals for publication, anticipating at least three papers to
include:
 a systematic review to identify the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness
 describing the nystagmus and any key distinguishing features between peripheral and central positional
nystagmus
 presenting the findings from the first 3D reconstructive imaging study of the inner ear
Conferences
I will submit these results to specialist conferences attended by doctors and members of the multidisciplinary team,
including the renowned International Barany Society, European Academy of Neurologists, and the British

sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets a diverse population in North Central London, with specific strategies for ethnic minorities and non-English speakers (e.g., interpreter provision). The patient advisory group includes diverse representation (ethnicities, ages), and the plan addresses barriers to participation such as language and travel costs.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While strategies for ethnic minorities and language barriers are strong, specific mitigation strategies for other underrepresented groups (e.g., those with severe disabilities preventing travel to the site) are less detailed, though travel reimbursement is mentioned.
sub_criteria.rationale: While strategies for ethnic minorities and language barriers are strong, specific mitigation strategies for other underrepresented groups (e.g., those with severe disabilities preventing travel to the site) are less detailed, though travel reimbursement is mentioned.

evidence.text:
and those that do not fulfil the
diagnostic criteria for BPPV or present with abnormalities in the eye examination referred for further review by a
specialist Neuro-otologist. It will expand on the systematic review to establish diagnostic differences between
peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness. It will also address critical gaps in the published literature on
the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV. The overall aim is to improve
accurate assessment and early treatment for patients with positional dizziness, whether that be due to peripheral
or central causes, priorities reported by the patient focus group.
Population
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, which includes white, Asian, and
Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London are Turkish, Irish, Polish, and Asian.
NCL is the second most deprived integrated care system in London19. These factors have been accounted for in
the development of this study, so that funding is available for participants travel and the provision of interpreters.
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The N

verse population,
will allow all those who present with positional dizziness to access assessment and treatment, to address the
study aims. The patient advisory group will provide guidance during the study to maximise recruitment from
under-represented groups to ensure the results are generalisable. Figure 1 summarises the flow of each
participant through the study duration.
Figure 1: Participant flow through the study.
Sample size
Dizziness has a lifetime prevalence of 35%1. Among a population of 490 patients with dizziness, 20% were
reported to have dizziness triggered by position changes7, of which 80% attributed to BPPV and 20% to central
causes. To ensure sufficient statistical power, a prevalence rate of 20% was used to calculate the required
sample size for this study investigating positional dizziness.
The sample size was calculated with the support of statistical advisors, using the formula n=[(z)2 (p)(1-p)]/(d)2,
where z is 1.96 for 95% confidence, p (prevalence) is 0.20 and d (margin of error) is 0.0522. This resulted in a
required sample size of 250 participants to estimate the prevalence of positional nystagmus with a ±5% precision.
Recruitment feasibility is supported

paper-based consent will be obtained and stored in a central REDCap database set up for this
project. Participants will then be invited to attend the hospital for assessment at the earliest point in time and their
GP informed of their participation.
Participant support
Potential participants can request the information sheet and consent form in paper format, as suggested by two
members of the patient focus group who did not have access to a computer. The signed paper copy of consent
will then be loaded into the secure database by the research team.
Potential participants who do not speak adequate English, will be able to either ask a family member to translate,
or request an interpreter is provided for them throughout this study. Access to an interpreter was included on
request of the patient focus group. Visual aids and a clear explanation of the manoeuvres and tests will be given
to each interpreter to translate.
To maximise participation of under-served groups24, participants will be able to select the time they visit the
hospital and travel costs will be remunerated promptly after each visit. Any non-attender will be contacted to
ensure any barriers to participation are mitiga

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Patients and the public have played a vital role in shaping this fellowship proposal. The study question and design
stem from recurring frustrations expressed by patients during routine clinical practice.
Subsequently, an online focus group of 10 patients, with a history of positional dizziness, shared their experiences.
Participants were from across the UK, due to University College London Hospital (UCLH) Trust’s tertiary service
reach, aged 26 to 78, representing diverse ethnicities, 6 females and 4 males. Six key themes emerged:
1. Delays in diagnosis – Patients often encounter delays, with dizziness in bed frequently misattributed to
BPPV without testing.
2. Challenges in treatment – A wide range of management approaches lead to inconsistent and sometimes
inadequate care.
3. Impact on quality of life – Positional dizziness causes significant frustration, anxiety, and disruption to
daily life.
4. Awareness gaps – Friends, family, and healthcare professionals often lack awareness that vestibular
migraine can mimic BPPV.
5. Communication barriers – Describing symptoms c

uses of positional dizziness, reduce delays and improve access to effective treatment, ultimately
enhancing patient outcomes and easing the burden on healthcare services.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
A patient advisory group, comprising eight members with experience of positional dizziness, aged 31 to 75
years, and representative of the study population (white, Asian, Black, and Turkish), was established through the
Ménière’s Disease Society, and will provide support throughout this study. Four online meetings a year will be
held, to reduce travel and time burdens. All members report access to an electronic device to take part and will be
remunerated for their time.
Roles and Contributions
1. Developing Resources
The group will review study advertisements and participant information sheets, ensuring they are accessible, free
from jargon, and culturally appropriate. They have a critical role to ensure potential participants understand the
study's purpose and feel comfortable engaging with it.
2. Recruitment
The group will help develop com

sub_criteria.name: Inclusivity Across the Research Lifecycle
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstration that inclusion is considered at all stages of the project: planning, recruitment, conduct, analysis, and dissemination.

signals.signal_text: References to inclusion in study design, participant selection, and analysis
Consideration of accessibility and equity in all project stages
Documented plans for ongoing evaluation and adaptation

sub_criteria.pros: Inclusion is considered across the entire research lifecycle. The plan includes monthly reviews of protected characteristics, adaptive recruitment strategies, and patient involvement from design through to dissemination. Accessibility is addressed via interpreters, travel costs, and flexible scheduling.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the lifecycle approach is well-documented and resourced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the lifecycle approach is well-documented and resourced.

evidence.text:
ted, with another appointment arranged if they wish to continue.
However, participants have the right to withdraw from the study at anytime without adversely affecting their
ongoing NHS care.
Data Management
Participants’ data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and scanning, but will be anonymised for any
dissemination. All data will be kept confidential and securely stored electronically, requiring a password to access
which will only be known by the research team. Data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine data
governance regulations.
Assessment visit
Participants will complete routine validated questionnaires used in dizziness and balance services (i.e. DHI, SVQ,
STAI-form Y, and VIMSSQ-short) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will help
identify any accompanying or distinguishing features of peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness.
Protected characteristics will also be collected to check for equality and inclusivity of those being recruited. These
will be reviewed monthly and discussed with the patient advisory group and The NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead, to help address any gaps in particip

ation. This may include adjusting how the study is
advertised or visiting specific clinics or teams in certain London boroughs.
Each assessment visit will take no more than one hour. It will include a lying and standing blood pressure check
for any postural hypotension, as the commonest non-vestibular cause of dizziness with position changes2 They
will then have a full eye movement examination in sitting using the video goggles. Positional tests3,8 using the
Rotundum chair will then be completed, whilst wearing the video goggles, to allow detailed analysis of the
nystagmus patterns to address the aims of work package 1 and 2.
criteria8,
Should BPPV be identified as per the diagnostic then the appropriate and established treatment
manoeuvre will be provided once, using the Rotundum chair. The treatments aim to relocate the displaced
otoconial debris from the affected semicircular canal, by moving the patient’s head and body through specific
positions. Each of these manoeuvres take no more than 3 minutes to complete.
Participants that do not fit the diagnostic criteria for the five main subtypes of BPPV in the positional tests8 or
present with abnormalities in the oculomotor examinat

uses of positional dizziness, reduce delays and improve access to effective treatment, ultimately
enhancing patient outcomes and easing the burden on healthcare services.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
A patient advisory group, comprising eight members with experience of positional dizziness, aged 31 to 75
years, and representative of the study population (white, Asian, Black, and Turkish), was established through the
Ménière’s Disease Society, and will provide support throughout this study. Four online meetings a year will be
held, to reduce travel and time burdens. All members report access to an electronic device to take part and will be
remunerated for their time.
Roles and Contributions
1. Developing Resources
The group will review study advertisements and participant information sheets, ensuring they are accessible, free
from jargon, and culturally appropriate. They have a critical role to ensure potential participants understand the
study's purpose and feel comfortable engaging with it.
2. Recruitment
The group will help develop com

munity-specific recruitment strategies to include wording in advertisements, to
maximise participation. This will occur prior to study commencement and at quarterly meetings when a summary
of the protected characteristics will be presented. They will help address gaps in participation, ensuring
underrepresented populations are included.
3. Dissemination
The group will collaborate to create dissemination materials to maximise the study’s impact. This includes
preparing plain English summaries for peer-reviewed journals, contributing to newsletters, developing an
infographic, social media posts, and public presentations.
Training and Support
To empower the advisory group to contribute effectively, training and support will be provided:
 Research design: Members will receive an overview of the study's objectives, methodologies, and
expected outcomes, ensuring they feel confident in their roles.
 Resource evaluation: Online training by the NIHR Learning for Involvement will cover best practices for
evaluating participant-facing materials, focusing on accessibility and inclusivity.
 Dissemination: Members will receive guidance from the NIHR Learning for Involvement, NIHR Evidence
and

Plain English Campaign’s websites on creating impactful plain English summaries, preparing
presentation materials, and using digital platforms such as social media.
In addition, throughout the project support will be offered, including time to address questions or challenges.
Impact and reporting
The public involvement in research impact toolkit (PIRIT) will be completed in collaboration with the patient
advisory group. This will help to plan, track and evaluate public involvement.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable.

A patient advisory group, comprising eight members with experience of positional dizziness, aged 31 to 75
years, and representative of the study population (white, Asian, Black, and Turkish), was established through the
Ménière’s Disease Society, and will provide support throughout this study. Four online meetings a year will be
held, to reduce travel and time burdens. All members report access to an electronic device to take part and will be
remunerated for their time.
Roles and Contributions
1. Developing Resources
The group will review study advertisements and participant information sheets, ensuring they are accessible, free
from jargon, and culturally appropriate. They have a critical role to ensure potential participants understand the
study's purpose and feel comfortable engaging with it.
2. Recruitment
The group will help develop com

munity-specific recruitment strategies to include wording in advertisements, to
maximise participation. This will occur prior to study commencement and at quarterly meetings when a summary
of the protected characteristics will be presented. They will help address gaps in participation, ensuring
underrepresented populations are included.
3. Dissemination
The group will collaborate to create dissemination materials to maximise the study’s impact. This includes
preparing plain English summaries for peer-reviewed journals, contributing to newsletters, developing an
infographic, social media posts, and public presentations.
Training and Support
To empower the advisory group to contribute effectively, training and support will be provided:
 Research design: Members will receive an overview of the study's objectives, methodologies, and
expected outcomes, ensuring they feel confident in their roles.
 Resource evaluation: Online training by the NIHR Learning for Involvement will cover best practices for
evaluating participant-facing materials, focusing on accessibility and inclusivity.

This research project was submitted to the Meniere’s Society, the UK’s balance and dizziness charity, to request
research funding. The application was peer reviewed by several external experts and layperson committee
members, and feedback was given to the trustees. A decision was given in December 2024 to award £55,106.04
towards the project. The award letter stated they felt the project was “…important and would be very beneficial for
patients with dizziness and unsteadiness”.
Unfortunately, as they are a small charity with limited funds and receive numerous grant applications each year,
they were unable to award the full amount requested. However, they are very enthusiastic about a joint
collaboration with the NIHR and fully support this research fellowship application. They are keen to help
throughout the project including with recruitment and dissemination via presentations, their newsletter and at their
balance awareness week events.
This fellowship application reflects the total cost of £447,139.00 that is required to undertake this research project,

analysing, findings to share with the patients and public, and professional manuscripts to be written.
I am committed to progressing my career as a clinical academic researcher, and my obligations to disseminate
results from my research activities. As such, I made the decision to leave my full-time NHS position to allow time
to focus on my research and took up a part time, honorary (unpaid) contract role based in the SENSE research
unit, at UCL. This part time role has allowed the time to prepare and successfully publish three articles relating to
the Lily Foundation research grant and assist in one other. It also allowed time to publish one more article relating
to this fellowship topic and be an editor for a standalone supplement in the Journal of laryngology and otology.
Moreover, alongside the research team, I continue to analyse the data to address secondary variables.
In addition, this part time role in the SENSE research unit has provided a rich opportunity to collaborate and
network with current UCL PhD students and other distinguished researchers. It has provided time to undertake
the patient focus group, establish a patient advisory group for this project, and the time t

Patient and public involvement
Ten people with experience of this type of dizziness, expressed frustrations in getting a diagnosis quickly, and
access to the right treatment. Their feedback has shaped the research question and study design, specifically:
 How to advertise the study
 Printing materials, instead of relying on email alone
 Providing interpreters
 Refunding travel costs
Another group of eight patients have agreed to help with recruitment and share the results widely.
Dissemination
Doctors and physiotherapists are the key audience to share these results with, to raise awareness of the causes
and treatment of dizziness caused by changing position, to improve diagnosis and treatment. This will be done
through publications in healthcare journals, presentations at international conferences and courses.

evidence.text:
eir efficacy3,8. These manoeuvres aim to
reposition displaced debris from the affected semicircular canal by moving the patient’s head and body through
specific positions. Each position leverages gravity to clear debris from the canal, assuming a standard canal
orientation across all individuals.
Mathematical modelling indicates there are anatomical variations in canal geometry, which may explain why
approximately 10% of patients do not respond to treatment17. However, no imaging studies have investigated if
there is a variation that could account for treatment non-response.
This unexplored area represents a critical gap in our understanding of BPPV management and those that do not
respond. Thus, in work package 3, a subgroup of 50 participants (see sample size section) will have exploratory
3-dimensional models created from MRI scans of the inner ear. Identifying an anatomical variation could inform
future studies and refine the treatment manoeuvre positions to improve treatment outcome, reduce falls risk and
mortality4,5. These were research priorities agreed by the patient focus group, to improve the speed and accuracy
of diagnosis and treatment.
4. What are the research questio

by recent data from the UCLH acute vertigo service, where 240 patients with
dizziness (48 with positional dizziness) were seen in six months. Additional sources, including GP surgeries,
community teams, and self-referrals, make this target achievable over 18 months.
Participant recruitment
The study will be advertised through the NCL Primary Care network and community services for North Central
London. This will include approximately 200 GP surgeries, and community neurology teams in Barnet, Camden,
Enfield, Haringey, and Islington. It will also be advertised in NCL emergency departments, NCL falls services and
internally at UCLH by email and attendance at departmental meetings. This broad recruitment strategy will ensure
the desired sample size will be met in the allocated recruitment time.
Advertisements will include screening questions for positional dizziness. These questions are adopted from a
diagnostic framework for vestibular causes of dizziness23, which was developed with patient involvement. Patients
guided the recruitment approach, and reported they would like an email and a telephone option on the
advertisements, both of which will be included.

As the host organisation, we are committed to providing the necessary infrastructure and support for the
successful completion of this fellowship. We recognise the importance of this fellowship in facilitating the
development of highly skilled clinical academic researchers who will make significant and direct contributions to
improving patient care. We value the importance of creating a research environment that is inclusive, as evidenced
by the range of subspecialty lab members, gender, and ethnicity within the team. We strive to ensure we provide a
supportive environment for all our lab members and collaborators, holding weekly meetings to discuss general lab
matters, offering an opportunity to evaluate research integrity, and offering 1-1 meetings with those that need

and technology in the gait laboratory at 33 Queen Square together, most notably the Rotundum chair that is

in behavioural neurology and vestibular disorders. The Department also benefits from direct links with experienced
researchers and clinicians at Queen Square and Royal Free hospitals to support her research. Ms Male shows

and anticipated impact
Development of diagnostic criteria
Key diagnostic features for central positional dizziness identified in this project will be presented locally to the NCL
primary care network, and nationally to the British Society of Neuro-Otology, for consultation. The findings will
then be shared internationally to the Barany Society in order to propose diagnostic criteria for central positional
dizziness and refine treatment recommendations for BPPV that doctors and physiotherapists can implement.
Publications
I will submit the results to relevant peer reviewed journals for publication, anticipating at least three papers to
include:
 a systematic review to identify the key diagnostic characteristics for central causes of positional dizziness
 describing the nystagmus and any key distinguishing features between peripheral and central positional
nystagmus
 presenting the findings from the first 3D reconstructive imaging study of the inner ear
Conferences
I will submit these results to specialist conferences attended by doctors and members of the multidisciplinary team,
including the renowned International Barany Society, European Academy of Neurologists, and the British

evidence.text:
and those that do not fulfil the
diagnostic criteria for BPPV or present with abnormalities in the eye examination referred for further review by a
specialist Neuro-otologist. It will expand on the systematic review to establish diagnostic differences between
peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness. It will also address critical gaps in the published literature on
the number of treatments required and recurrence rates for each subtype of BPPV. The overall aim is to improve
accurate assessment and early treatment for patients with positional dizziness, whether that be due to peripheral
or central causes, priorities reported by the patient focus group.
Population
The North Central London (NCL) area is home to a diverse range of ethnicities, which includes white, Asian, and
Black people. The largest ethnic minority communities in this area of London are Turkish, Irish, Polish, and Asian.
NCL is the second most deprived integrated care system in London19. These factors have been accounted for in
the development of this study, so that funding is available for participants travel and the provision of interpreters.
The study will be conducted at a single site, based at The N

verse population,
will allow all those who present with positional dizziness to access assessment and treatment, to address the
study aims. The patient advisory group will provide guidance during the study to maximise recruitment from
under-represented groups to ensure the results are generalisable. Figure 1 summarises the flow of each
participant through the study duration.
Figure 1: Participant flow through the study.
Sample size
Dizziness has a lifetime prevalence of 35%1. Among a population of 490 patients with dizziness, 20% were
reported to have dizziness triggered by position changes7, of which 80% attributed to BPPV and 20% to central
causes. To ensure sufficient statistical power, a prevalence rate of 20% was used to calculate the required
sample size for this study investigating positional dizziness.
The sample size was calculated with the support of statistical advisors, using the formula n=[(z)2 (p)(1-p)]/(d)2,
where z is 1.96 for 95% confidence, p (prevalence) is 0.20 and d (margin of error) is 0.0522. This resulted in a
required sample size of 250 participants to estimate the prevalence of positional nystagmus with a ±5% precision.

Participant support
Potential participants can request the information sheet and consent form in paper format, as suggested by two
members of the patient focus group who did not have access to a computer. The signed paper copy of consent

Potential participants who do not speak adequate English, will be able to either ask a family member to translate,
or request an interpreter is provided for them throughout this study. Access to an interpreter was included on

To maximise participation of under-served groups24, participants will be able to select the time they visit the
hospital and travel costs will be remunerated promptly after each visit. Any non-attender will be contacted to
