Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
Patients and the public have played a vital role in shaping this fellowship proposal. The study question and design
stem from recurring frustrations expressed by patients during routine clinical practice.
Subsequently, an online focus group of 10 patients, with a history of positional dizziness, shared their experiences.
Participants were from across the UK, due to University College London Hospital (UCLH) Trust’s tertiary service
reach, aged 26 to 78, representing diverse ethnicities, 6 females and 4 males. Six key themes emerged:
1. Delays in diagnosis – Patients often encounter delays, with dizziness in bed frequently misattributed to
BPPV without testing.
2. Challenges in treatment – A wide range of management approaches lead to inconsistent and sometimes
inadequate care.
3. Impact on quality of life – Positional dizziness causes significant frustration, anxiety, and disruption to
daily life.
4. Awareness gaps – Friends, family, and healthcare professionals often lack awareness that vestibular
migraine can mimic BPPV.
5. Communication barriers – Describing symptoms c

uses of positional dizziness, reduce delays and improve access to effective treatment, ultimately
enhancing patient outcomes and easing the burden on healthcare services.

However, participants have the right to withdraw from the study at anytime without adversely affecting their

Data Management
Participants’ data will be identifiable for the purpose of follow up, and scanning, but will be anonymised for any
dissemination. All data will be kept confidential and securely stored electronically, requiring a password to access
which will only be known by the research team. Data will be handled securely in line with UCLH routine data
governance regulations.
Assessment visit
Participants will complete routine validated questionnaires used in dizziness and balance services (i.e. DHI, SVQ,
STAI-form Y, and VIMSSQ-short) and provide more personal information relating to their health. This will help
identify any accompanying or distinguishing features of peripheral and central causes of positional dizziness.
Protected characteristics will also be collected to check for equality and inclusivity of those being recruited. These
will be reviewed monthly and discussed with the patient advisory group and The NCL Community
Neuroscience project lead, to help address any gaps in particip

ation. This may include adjusting how the study is
advertised or visiting specific clinics or teams in certain London boroughs.
Each assessment visit will take no more than one hour. It will include a lying and standing blood pressure check
for any postural hypotension, as the commonest non-vestibular cause of dizziness with position changes2 They
will then have a full eye movement examination in sitting using the video goggles. Positional tests3,8 using the
Rotundum chair will then be completed, whilst wearing the video goggles, to allow detailed analysis of the
nystagmus patterns to address the aims of work package 1 and 2.

Should BPPV be identified as per the diagnostic then the appropriate and established treatment
manoeuvre will be provided once, using the Rotundum chair. The treatments aim to relocate the displaced
otoconial debris from the affected semicircular canal, by moving the patient’s head and body through specific
positions. Each of these manoeuvres take no more than 3 minutes to complete.

Training and Support

 Research design: Members will receive an overview of the study's objectives, methodologies, and

 Resource evaluation: Online training by the NIHR Learning for Involvement will cover best practices for

In addition, throughout the project support will be offered, including time to address questions or challenges.
Impact and reporting
The public involvement in research impact toolkit (PIRIT) will be completed in collaboration with the patient

Ménière’s Disease Society, and will provide support throughout this study. Four online meetings a year will be
held, to reduce travel and time burdens. All members report access to an electronic device to take part and will be
remunerated for their time.
Roles and Contributions
1. Developing Resources
The group will review study advertisements and participant information sheets, ensuring they are accessible, free
from jargon, and culturally appropriate. They have a critical role to ensure potential participants understand the
study's purpose and feel comfortable engaging with it.
2. Recruitment
The group will help develop com

munity-specific recruitment strategies to include wording in advertisements, to
maximise participation. This will occur prior to study commencement and at quarterly meetings when a summary
of the protected characteristics will be presented. They will help address gaps in participation, ensuring
underrepresented populations are included.
3. Dissemination
The group will collaborate to create dissemination materials to maximise the study’s impact. This includes
preparing plain English summaries for peer-reviewed journals, contributing to newsletters, developing an
infographic, social media posts, and public presentations.
Training and Support
To empower the advisory group to contribute effectively, training and support will be provided:
 Research design: Members will receive an overview of the study's objectives, methodologies, and
expected outcomes, ensuring they feel confident in their roles.
 Resource evaluation: Online training by the NIHR Learning for Involvement will cover best practices for
evaluating participant-facing materials, focusing on accessibility and inclusivity.
 Dissemination: Members will receive guidance from the NIHR Learning for Involvement, NIHR Evidence
and

findings.implication: Applicant CV: Lists MRes, BSc, and specialized vestibular courses, demonstrating formal training and relevant clinical experience.
Applicant CV: Extensive publication record (2018-2024) in neurology and otology journals, plus a research grant, indicating strong research output.
Applicant CV: Progression from Junior to Senior Physiotherapist and Honorary Research Associate shows increasing responsibility and leadership.
Applicant CV: Current role as Vestibular Physiotherapist alongside research publications demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic career path.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a vision to become a clinical academic leader and outlines a trajectory from clinical practice to a PhD fellowship.
Applicant Research Background: Explains the need for the award to secure dedicated time for research, which was previously unavailable in full-time NHS roles, enabling career progression.
Applicant Research Background: Details extensive experience in study design, data analysis, and mixed-methods research, directly relevant to the proposed doctoral work.
Applicant Research Background: Lists multiple peer-reviewed publications in high-impact journals and a secured research grant, demonstrating strong research output.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates leadership through service development, committee chairmanship, mentoring students, and co-editing journal supplements.
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly states the goal of becoming a clinical academic leader and details the integration of clinical practice with research activities.
Applicant Research Background: Highlights potential to enhance patient quality of life and alleviate NHS strain, supported by positive feedback from a charity regarding patient benefit.
Applicant Research Background: Provides a clear total cost breakdown and details existing funding from the Meniere's Society, justifying the remaining resource request.
Applicant Research Background: Mentions establishing a patient advisory group and conducting patient focus groups, indicating integration of lived experience.
Applicant Research Background: Outlines plans for dissemination via charity newsletters, awareness events, and peer-reviewed publications to maximize public benefit.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates the problem, aims, methods, and beneficiaries in accessible language, supporting pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies specific knowledge gaps regarding brain causes, treatment frequency, and non-responders, supporting pr.2.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines three distinct studies with defined sample sizes (250 and 25), indicating feasible scope, supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific methods including literature review, questionnaires, assessments, and 3D inner ear scans, supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details patient involvement in shaping study design, advertising, and recruitment, supporting pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Mentions providing interpreters and refunding travel costs to ensure inclusive participation, supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly states patient feedback shaped the research question and study design, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines dissemination to clinicians and patients via journals, conferences, social media, and workshops, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly identifies knowledge gaps regarding central causes, treatment recurrence, and anatomical variations, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines three distinct studies with specific sample sizes (250 and 25), indicating a feasible scope aligned with award expectations.
Scientific Abstract: Details specific methods including systematic review, video-goggle assessments, and 3D MRI reconstruction, demonstrating methodological rigor.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights impact on patient quality of life, falls risk, and clinician guidelines, clearly articulating potential benefits.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions patient focus groups informing challenges and plans for patient workshops, indicating engagement with people and communities.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions recruiting adults in North Central London, though specific inclusive strategies are less detailed here than in the Plain English Summary.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly identifies critical gaps in literature regarding treatment frequency, recurrence rates, and anatomical variations causing non-response, supporting pr.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Defines three work packages with specific sample sizes (250 and 25) and an 18-month timeline, demonstrating feasible scope aligned with award expectations.
Detailed Research Plan: Details rigorous methods including PICO framework for systematic review, Rotundum chair for standardized assessment, and 3D MRI reconstruction, supporting pr.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Articulates clear benefits including reduced falls risk, improved diagnostic accuracy, and reduced NHS strain, directly supporting pr.5.
Detailed Research Plan: Describes active involvement of a patient advisory group in shaping recruitment, consent processes, and dissemination strategies, supporting pr.6.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines specific inclusive strategies such as interpreter provision, travel cost remuneration, and flexible scheduling to support under-served groups, supporting pr.9.
Detailed Research Plan: Demonstrates integration of lived experience through patient focus groups influencing study design, recruitment materials, and dissemination formats, supporting wp.1.
Detailed Research Plan: Details a comprehensive dissemination plan including peer-reviewed papers, conferences, and patient-facing infographics to maximize public benefit, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Targets a diverse population in North Central London with specific strategies for ethnic minorities and non-English speakers, supporting wp.4.
Detailed Research Plan: Includes plans for monthly review of protected characteristics and adaptive recruitment strategies to ensure inclusivity across the lifecycle, supporting wp.5.
Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly allocates resources for interpreters and travel costs to support inclusion, indicating a costed plan for research inclusion, supporting wp.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details how patient focus groups shaped the study question, design, and plain English summary, with specific changes incorporated based on feedback.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines a comprehensive dissemination plan involving the patient advisory group in creating plain English summaries, infographics, and social media posts.

Patient & Public Involvement: Highlights diverse representation in the advisory group (ethnicities, ages) and specific strategies to address communication barriers and include underrepresented populations.

Patient & Public Involvement: Describes involvement across the lifecycle from design and recruitment to dissemination, with quarterly reviews of protected characteristics to ensure ongoing inclusivity.
Patient & Public Involvement: Confirms members will be remunerated and provided with training (NIHR Learning for Involvement), indicating a resourced plan for inclusion.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details a comprehensive training plan including UCL workshops, external courses in systematic reviews, epidemiology, and data analysis, directly supporting td.1.

Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly identifies skill gaps (e.g., handling large clinical datasets, 3D modelling) and maps specific courses and mentorship to address them, supporting td.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Links training in implementation science and leadership to career goals of becoming a clinical academic leader and securing future fellowships, supporting td.3.
Training & Development and Research Support: Highlights supervisors' strong track records, including PhD completions, significant grant funding, and high-impact publications, supporting ss.1.
Training & Development and Research Support: Describes structured supervision with monthly meetings, 1:1 sessions, and specific mentorship on study design and statistics, supporting ss.2.

Training & Development and Research Support: Host organisation confirms access to world-leading facilities (Rotundum chair, gait lab) and a clear pathway for post-doctoral progression, supporting ss.3.
Training & Development and Research Support: Host statement and supervisor profiles emphasize commitment to EDI, inclusive culture, and research integrity training, supporting ss.4.
Training & Development and Research Support: Host statement notes the diversity of the SENSE Research Unit team across gender and ethnicity, supporting wp.2.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed justifications linking specific costs (salary, MRI, questionnaires) to research tasks and work packages, supporting pr.7.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates specific funds for travel reimbursement and interpreters to ensure equality and inclusivity for diverse participants, supporting pr.9.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes costed lines for patient advisory group remuneration, workshops, and dissemination materials, demonstrating a resourced plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details specific training courses (systematic review, statistics, Python, implementation science) with costs and justifications linking them to project needs, supporting td.1.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Maps training activities (e.g., data analytics, overseas visits for specialist equipment) directly to skill gaps required for work packages 2 and 3, supporting td.2.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Links training in implementation science and epidemiology to the applicant's NIHR academic pathway and future career goals, supporting td.3.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show strong readability (Flesch-Kincaid 7.59, 0% long sentences) and low jargon density (3.58%), with coverage of all required summary components, supporting pr.1.

Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan and Scientific Abstract, providing structural evidence for evidence review and gap identification checks.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan and SUMMARY BUDGET, providing structural evidence for scope and feasibility checks.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Detailed Research Plan with high word count, providing structural evidence for methodological rigor checks.

Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Patient & Public Involvement section, providing structural evidence for engagement checks.
